questions.scenario: A CREST member is the registered owner of 100,000 shares in BT Group plc. It holds 50,000 of those shares for a broker. The broker acquired its interest in the 50,000 shares on behalf of a private investor.
questions.stem: Which one of the following statements correctly describes the private investor's position in relation to the 50,000 shares?

options.A: The investor has a legal and equitable interest in the shares.
options.B: The investor does not have any interest in the shares.
options.C: The investor is the legal owner of the shares.
options.D: The investor has an equitable interest in the shares but not the beneficial interest.
options.E: The investor has an equitable and beneficial interest in the shares.

questions.explanation: E is correct. The CREST member is the registered legal owner. The broker holds the 50,000 shares on trust for the investor — the broker has an equitable interest but not the beneficial interest (it holds for the investor, not itself). The investor is therefore the equitable and beneficial owner of the shares. B: the investor clearly has an interest — option B states the investor has no interest, which is wrong. C: this describes the broker's position, not the investor's — the investor has the beneficial interest. A: the investor has no legal interest — that is held by the CREST member, not the broker. D: the investor has the full beneficial interest, not the broker who merely holds equitably for the investor.

questions.scenario: A testator leaves a will stating: 'I give all my property to my wife, feeling confident that she will act justly to our children in dividing the same.' The wife claims the estate absolutely.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether a trust has been created?

options.A: A trust has been created because the wife is under a moral obligation to benefit the children.
options.B: No trust has been created — the words 'feeling confident' are precatory and impose no legally binding obligation on the wife.
options.C: A trust has been created because the testator clearly intended to benefit the children.
options.D: No trust has been created because the testator failed to name a trustee.
options.E: A trust has been created by the use of the word 'confident', which indicates a clear intention.

questions.explanation: B is correct. 'Feeling confident' is precatory language — it expresses a hope but imposes no legally binding obligation. Following Mussoorie Bank v Raynor (1882) and Re Adams & Kensington Vestry [1884], such words do not create a trust. The wife takes absolutely. A: the testator's desire to benefit the children is clear, but the words used do not impose a legal obligation. C: a moral obligation does not create a legal trust. E: using the word 'confident' does not make language imperative. D: absence of a named trustee does not prevent a trust failing for other reasons.

questions.scenario: Mr Constance, who was in a relationship with Mrs Paul, repeatedly said to her 'this money is as much yours as mine' in relation to a joint bank account. The couple used the account for joint purposes, including depositing joint winnings. Mr Constance died and his executor claimed the account belonged solely to his estate.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether a trust was created?

options.A: No trust was created because Mr Constance never transferred the account to a trustee.
options.B: A trust was created — the repeated informal words and conduct treating the money as jointly owned were sufficient to constitute a declaration of trust in favour of Mrs Paul.
options.C: No trust was created because informal statements cannot create legally binding trusts.
options.D: A trust was created because the account was used for joint purposes, which automatically creates a constructive trust.
options.E: No trust was created because the account was in Mr Constance's sole name and he never used the word 'trust'.

questions.explanation: B is correct, following Paul v Constance [1977]. The Court of Appeal held that informal words — 'this money is as much yours as mine' — combined with conduct treating the fund as jointly owned, were sufficient to constitute a declaration of trust. No technical language is required; substance prevails over form. A: use of the word 'trust' is not required. C: informal words can create a legally binding trust on their true construction. D: joint use alone does not automatically create a constructive trust — there must be a common intention. E: a self-declaration of trust does not require transfer.

questions.scenario: A settlor declares a trust over '50 of my 200 ordinary shares in ABC plc', all of the same class, without specifying which particular share certificates will form the trust fund.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether there is certainty of subject matter?

options.A: There is certainty of subject matter only if the shares are first transferred into a separate account.
options.B: There is no certainty of subject matter because the beneficial entitlement requirement cannot be satisfied without identifying specific shares.
options.C: There is no certainty of subject matter because the specific shares are not identified or segregated.
options.D: There is no certainty of subject matter because a trust cannot be declared over a fraction of a larger holding.
options.E: There is certainty of subject matter — intangible fungible assets of the same class do not require segregation to be identifiable.

questions.explanation: E is correct, following Hunter v Moss [1994]. The Court of Appeal held that intangible, fungible assets of the same class — such as shares — are wholly interchangeable. Unlike physical goods (Re London Wine Co), segregation is not required to identify the trust property. C: this reasoning applies to tangible goods, not identical intangible shares. D: a declaration over a fraction of a larger holding is valid for fungible intangibles. A: no transfer to a separate account is required for a self-declaration. B: beneficial entitlement can be satisfied where the class of assets is certain.

questions.scenario: A testator's will purports to create a trust over 'the bulk of my residuary estate' for his grandchildren.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether there is certainty of subject matter?

options.A: There is no certainty of subject matter — although the residuary estate is ascertainable, 'the bulk' of it is not a precise or calculable amount.
options.B: There is certainty of subject matter if the executor can make a reasonable estimate of the bulk.
options.C: There is certainty of subject matter because the residuary estate can be identified with precision.
options.D: There is no certainty of subject matter because it is not possible to identify the grandchildren.
options.E: There is certainty of subject matter because 'bulk' is a commonly understood word and the courts will give it its natural meaning.

questions.explanation: A is correct. In Palmer v Simmonds (1854), 'the bulk of my residuary estate' was held to be uncertain — while the residuary estate itself is calculable, 'the bulk' does not identify a precise portion and could mean different things to different people. There is accordingly no certainty of subject matter. C: the residuary estate as a whole may be ascertainable, but 'the bulk' of it is not a precise amount. E: courts do not substitute their own view of a vague phrase to save a failing trust. D: uncertainty of objects is a separate issue. B: estimation is not the appropriate test — precision is required.

questions.scenario: A settlor creates a fixed trust directing trustees to distribute a fund equally among 'all my old friends'. No further definition of 'friends' is given.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether there is certainty of objects?

options.A: There is certainty of objects if the settlor leaves a personal list of friends in their estate.
options.B: There is certainty of objects because the trustees can identify who the settlor's friends are from personal knowledge.
options.C: There is certainty of objects because 'friends' is a commonly understood concept.
options.D: There is certainty of objects because the trustees can use their discretion to identify friends.
options.E: There is no certainty of objects — a fixed trust requires a complete list of all beneficiaries, and 'friends' is conceptually uncertain so no such list can be compiled.

questions.explanation: E is correct. A fixed trust requires the complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages [1955]): it must be possible to draw up a complete list of all beneficiaries. 'Friends' (and 'old friends') is conceptually uncertain — there is no precise legal definition — so a complete list cannot be drawn up, and the trust fails. B: personal knowledge does not cure conceptual uncertainty. A: ordinary usage does not give 'friends' the precision equity requires. D: trustees have no discretion in a fixed trust — they must distribute equally among all members of the class. C: a posthumous list of friends might provide evidential clarity but does not cure conceptual uncertainty.

questions.scenario: A settlor creates a discretionary trust for the benefit of 'such of the employees and ex-employees of my company and their relatives and dependants as the trustees shall select'.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the applicable test and likely outcome?

options.A: The trust is valid but only for the employees and ex-employees sub-class; the trust fails for relatives and dependants.
options.B: The complete list test applies to discretionary trusts and the trust fails because a complete list cannot be drawn up.
options.C: The is/is not test applies but 'dependants' is conceptually uncertain and the trust therefore fails.
options.D: The complete list test applies — the trustees must be able to draw up a complete list of all possible objects, and 'relatives and dependants' make this impossible, so the trust fails.
options.E: The is/is not test applies (McPhail v Doulton) — the trust is valid because it can be said with certainty whether any given person is or is not a member of the class; 'employees', 'relatives' and 'dependants' are all conceptually certain.

questions.explanation: E is correct. In McPhail v Doulton [1971], the House of Lords held that discretionary trusts are subject to the is/is not test, not the complete list test. 'Employees', 'ex-employees', 'relatives' (Re Baden No 2 — descendants of a common ancestor) and 'dependants' (those financially reliant on another) are all sufficiently conceptually certain. B: the complete list test applies to fixed trusts only. C: 'dependants' was held to be conceptually certain in Re Baden No 2 [1973]. D: this states the pre-McPhail position which was overruled. A: there is no basis to sever — the whole class is valid.

questions.scenario: A man orally declares that he holds the house registered in his sole name on trust for himself and his partner in equal shares. No written document is signed.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the validity of this declaration?

options.A: The declaration is valid because equity will not allow a statute to be used as an instrument of fraud.
options.B: The declaration is invalid because a trust of land must be created by deed.
options.C: The declaration is valid because a self-declaration of trust does not require any formality.
options.D: The declaration is valid because it relates to the man's own property and no transfer is needed.
options.E: The declaration is invalid — under s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925, a declaration of trust in respect of land must be manifested and proved by signed writing; an oral declaration is unenforceable.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Section 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 requires that any declaration of trust respecting land or any interest therein must be manifested and proved by some writing signed by some person who is able to declare such trust. An oral declaration is unenforceable (though not void). C: the self-declaration nature is irrelevant — s 53(1)(b) applies regardless. D: self-ownership is irrelevant — the writing requirement applies to all declarations of trust over land. B: a deed is required to convey legal title (s 52 LPA 1925) — a trust declaration requires writing but not a deed. A: while equity may impose a constructive trust in fraud cases (exempt under s 53(2)), this requires additional facts; a bare oral declaration is simply unenforceable.

questions.scenario: A settlor executes a deed purporting to transfer shares to trustees to hold on an express trust for named beneficiaries. The shares are not transferred by the completion of a stock transfer form and no new name is entered in the company's register of members.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the trust has been constituted?

options.A: The trust has been constituted because the beneficiaries have provided consideration.
options.B: The trust has been constituted because the trustees have possession of the deed.
options.C: The trust has not been constituted — the correct method for transferring shares is a stock transfer form plus registration; executing a deed alone does not pass legal title.
options.D: The trust has been constituted because the settlor's death prevents any revocation.
options.E: The trust has been constituted because the settlor executed a valid deed transferring the shares.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Each type of property has its own method of transfer. For shares, legal title passes only when the transferee is registered in the company's register of members, following completion of a stock transfer form (Milroy v Lord [1862]). A deed alone is the incorrect method and does not constitute the trust. E: executing a deed is not the correct method for shares. B: possession of the deed by trustees is irrelevant to constitution. A: beneficiaries in an express trust are typically volunteers — consideration is irrelevant to constitution. D: death is irrelevant — the trust was not constituted during the settlor's lifetime.

questions.scenario: On 1 March, a settlor executes a valid stock transfer form and supporting documentation to transfer shares to trustees, and hands these to the company registrar. She dies on 15 March. On 30 March, the company registers the transfer.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the trust has been constituted?

options.A: The trust was constituted on 30 March when the shares were registered, but only the administrators of the estate, not the trustees, can benefit from this.
options.B: The trust was constituted on 1 March — once the settlor had done everything in her power, including executing the stock transfer form, constitution is treated as complete even if registration occurred after death.
options.C: The trust is constituted only if the trustees provide consideration.
options.D: The trust has not been constituted because the settlor's death terminated any intention to create a trust.
options.E: The trust has not been constituted because legal title to the shares had not passed at the date of death.

questions.explanation: B is correct, applying Re Rose [1952]. The Court of Appeal held that where a transferor has done everything within their own power to effect a transfer — including executing the relevant transfer form — constitution is treated as complete from the moment the transferor did all they could. Registration by a third party (the company) does not have to precede death for the transfer to be effective. A: Re Rose directly contradicts this — constitution does not require registration to have occurred during the settlor's lifetime. D: Re Rose means the trust was constituted on 1 March, before the settlor's death, not on 30 March. E: intention is fixed at the time of the act — death does not undo a completed transfer. C: consideration is not required to constitute a trust.

questions.scenario: A grandmother promises her grandson: 'I will leave you the farm in my will.' In reliance on this promise, the grandson works on the farm for ten years without wages, turning down other employment opportunities. The grandmother dies intestate.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the grandson's position?

options.A: The grandson has no legal claim because the grandmother's promise was a future gift and equity will not perfect an imperfect gift.
options.B: The grandson has a contractual claim for the value of his labour.
options.C: The grandson has no claim because a promise in a will can always be revoked.
options.D: The grandson may have a claim based on proprietary estoppel — if he can show a clear assurance, detrimental reliance, and that it would be unconscionable to deny the assurance, the court may grant a remedy.
options.E: The grandson has a claim based on constructive trust because the farm was promised to him.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Proprietary estoppel may arise where: (1) the landowner makes an assurance (the promise to leave the farm); (2) the claimant relies on it to their detriment (10 years' unpaid labour and foregone opportunities); and (3) it would be unconscionable to deny the assurance (Thorner v Major [2009]; Gillet v Holt [2001]). The remedy is discretionary and may include a charge, licence, or transfer of the land. A: proprietary estoppel is a recognised exception to the 'equity will not perfect an imperfect gift' rule. E: a constructive trust is one possible remedy but the basis of the claim is proprietary estoppel. C: a promise acted upon to the claimant's detriment can give rise to an irrevocable equity. B: there was no contract — the grandson worked without any enforceable agreement for wages.

questions.scenario: B is the beneficiary of a trust of shares, holding an equitable interest. Without any writing, B orally instructs the trustee to hold the shares on trust for C instead. The trustee agrees.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether this is effective?

options.A: The oral instruction is effective because B is the beneficial owner and can deal with her interest as she chooses.
options.B: The oral instruction is effective if the trustee consents in writing.
options.C: The oral instruction creates a constructive trust in favour of C, which is exempt from formality requirements.
options.D: The oral instruction is effective because B is directing the trustee, not disposing of her own interest.
options.E: The oral instruction is ineffective — under s 53(1)(c) LPA 1925, a disposition of a subsisting equitable interest must be in writing signed by the disponor; failure renders it void, not merely unenforceable.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Section 53(1)(c) LPA 1925 requires that any disposition of a subsisting equitable interest must be in writing and signed by the person making it (or their agent). An oral instruction is void (not merely unenforceable) — confirmed in Grey v IRC [1960]. A: beneficial ownership does not exempt a disposition from s 53(1)(c). D: directing the trustee to hold for C is precisely a disposition of B's equitable interest (Grey v IRC). B: the trustee's written consent does not satisfy s 53(1)(c) — the writing must come from B. C: a genuine constructive trust would require facts beyond an oral direction; on these facts s 53(1)(c) renders the disposition void.

questions.scenario: A testator's will creates a trust to 'maintain and care for my horses for as long as they shall live'. There are no human beneficiaries identified.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the validity of this purpose trust?

options.A: The trust is void because it violates the rule against perpetuities.
options.B: The trust is valid because it is for a specific, limited purpose that can be fulfilled.
options.C: The trust is valid because trusts for animals are an established exception to the beneficiary principle.
options.D: The trust is void — the beneficiary principle requires a trust to have ascertainable beneficiaries who can enforce it; a trust for animals has no such beneficiaries.
options.E: The trust is valid because it falls within the Re Denley exception.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The beneficiary principle (Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804]) requires that every trust has a beneficiary capable of enforcing it. A trust for animals has no such beneficiary and is therefore void. While cases such as Re Dean (1889) permitted animal trusts as anomalous exceptions, that decision is controversial and not universally followed. B: a limited purpose alone does not validate a purpose trust without beneficiaries capable of enforcement. C: animal trusts are a narrow anomalous exception, not an established general category — this overstates the position. E: Re Denley applies where human beneficiaries can enforce the trust — it does not extend to animal trusts. A: perpetuity is a separate issue; the primary reason for failure is the absence of a human beneficiary.

questions.scenario: A settlor wishes to establish a trust to fund research into a controversial political theory and to disseminate its findings as widely as possible.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether this is a valid charitable trust?

options.A: The trust is charitable because any research and dissemination of knowledge advances education.
options.B: The trust is not charitable because political research cannot be educational.
options.C: The trust is charitable because the Charities Act 2011 recognises the advancement of education broadly.
options.D: The trust is charitable provided the research is conducted by qualified academics.
options.E: The trust is not charitable for the advancement of education if its primary purpose is to promote a particular political viewpoint rather than provide balanced, objective educational benefit.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Under the Charities Act 2011, the advancement of education is a charitable purpose, but all charitable trusts must satisfy the public benefit requirement (s 4 CA 2011). A trust whose primary purpose is to promote or advocate a particular political viewpoint does not satisfy public benefit — it is partisan rather than educational (Re Hopkinson [1949]; McGovern v AG [1982]). C: education requires balanced, objective inquiry — promotion of a viewpoint does not satisfy this. A: the Act requires both charitable purpose and public benefit; promotion of a political theory fails on public benefit. D: the qualifications of researchers do not determine charitable status. B: overstated — political research can be educational if it satisfies public benefit.

questions.scenario: A trust is established to provide financial assistance exclusively to 'poor and distressed members of the ABC employees' social club'.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether this trust is charitable?

options.A: The trust is not charitable because it only benefits members of a private club, which is not a section of the public.
options.B: The trust is charitable — trusts for the relief of poverty are treated differently from other charitable purposes and can benefit a defined private group without failing the public benefit test.
options.C: The trust is charitable because the relief of poverty is always a charitable purpose regardless of the class of beneficiaries.
options.D: The trust is not charitable because the beneficiaries must be members of the general public.
options.E: The trust is charitable only if the ABC Ltd employees include members of the general public.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The relief of poverty occupies a special position in charity law. Unlike other charitable purposes, a trust for the relief of poverty among a defined group — even a private group such as employees or members of a club — can satisfy the public benefit requirement (Re Scarisbrick [1951]; Dingle v Turner [1972]). The rationale is that poverty relief is so important a public good that the requirement is relaxed. D: this is the general public benefit rule, but poverty is the exception. C: overstated — while poverty trusts are treated more generously, there are still limits. E: the general public requirement is modified for poverty trusts. A: the composition of the group is not decisive for poverty trusts.

questions.scenario: A trust is established to provide and maintain a cricket ground and pavilion for use by the employees of a large firm, who pay a subscription fee to use it.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether this is a valid charitable trust?

options.A: The trust is charitable because the Recreational Charities Act 1958 makes all recreation facilities charitable.
options.B: The trust is charitable provided the employees can invite members of the public.
options.C: The trust is not charitable because cricket is not a sufficiently important purpose to warrant charitable status.
options.D: The trust is not charitable — although recreation may be a charitable purpose, a trust restricted to employees of a single firm benefits only a private class and fails the public benefit test.
options.E: The trust is charitable because sport and recreation promote public health.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The advancement of amateur sport can be charitable under the Charities Act 2011, but all charitable trusts must satisfy the public benefit requirement (s 4). A trust restricted exclusively to employees of a single firm benefits only a private class and therefore fails public benefit (IRC v City of Glasgow Police Athletic Association [1953]; Re Nottage [1895]). E: promoting health can be a charitable purpose, but the public benefit requirement must still be satisfied. A: overstated — the Recreational Charities Act 1958 did not make all recreation charitable; public benefit must still be shown. B: the ability to invite guests does not transform a private employee facility into a public benefit. C: the sport itself is not the issue — public benefit is.

questions.scenario: A trust is created for the purpose of maintaining a sports ground for the use and benefit of employees of a company, for so long as the company carries on business.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether this trust is valid?

options.A: The trust is void because purpose trusts are always void — equity requires a beneficiary who can enforce the trust, and a bare purpose without ascertainable beneficiaries can never satisfy the beneficiary principle.
options.B: The trust is valid because the employees' consent creates a contractual obligation.
options.C: The trust is charitable because the Charities Act 2011 recognises sport and recreation as charitable purposes, and the employees of a company constitute a sufficient section of the public.
options.D: The trust is void because it has no ascertainable human beneficiaries.
options.E: The trust may be valid under Re Denley [1969] — where a purpose trust directly benefits ascertainable individuals who can enforce it, the beneficiary principle is satisfied.

questions.explanation: E is correct. In Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969], Goff J held that where the performance of a purpose directly or indirectly benefits ascertainable individuals, those individuals have standing to enforce the trust and the beneficiary principle is therefore satisfied. The employees here are the ascertainable beneficiaries who can enforce the trust. A: the employees are the indirect beneficiaries who can enforce — this satisfies the beneficiary principle. C: restricting benefit to employees of a single firm fails public benefit — not charitable. D: overstated — Re Denley carves out an important exception. B: the employees have no contract with the trustees — the basis of validity is Re Denley, not contract.

questions.scenario: A charitable trust was established to fund research into a specific disease. A cure for the disease is subsequently discovered and published. The trust still holds significant surplus funds.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes what happens to the remaining funds?

options.A: The trust continues indefinitely because charitable trusts are perpetual.
options.B: The remaining funds must be returned to the settlor's estate because the charitable purpose has failed.
options.C: The court or Charity Commission may apply the cy-pres doctrine — where a charitable purpose becomes impossible, the funds may be directed to another purpose as near as possible to the original.
options.D: The trustees may use the funds for any purpose they consider charitable.
options.E: The remaining funds pass to the Crown as bona vacantia because charitable trusts cannot result back to the settlor.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The cy-pres doctrine (Charities Act 2011, ss 62-63) applies where a charitable purpose becomes impossible, impractical, or has been achieved. The court or Charity Commission may make a scheme directing the funds to another charitable purpose that is as close as possible to the original intention. E: charitable trusts do not result back to settlors in the way private trusts do — the cy-pres doctrine applies instead. B: bona vacantia applies only where cy-pres cannot be applied (e.g. where the gift was never charitable, or where the purpose was never established). D: trustees cannot unilaterally choose any charitable purpose — a court or Charity Commission scheme is required. A: the trust does not continue automatically when its purpose is achieved — surplus funds must be dealt with.

questions.scenario: Two lay trustees of a family trust invest the trust fund entirely in shares of a single technology company, believing it to be a 'dead cert'. They do not seek professional advice. The shares fall dramatically in value.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the trustees' position?

options.A: The trustees are not liable because investment decisions involve commercial judgment which courts do not second-guess.
options.B: The trustees are liable only if it can be proved they knew the shares would fall.
options.C: The trustees are liable for breach of their duty of care — s 1 TA 2000 required reasonable care and skill, s 4 required diversification, and s 5 required proper investment advice before concentrating the fund in one stock.
options.D: The trustees are not liable because they are lay trustees and held to a lower standard of care.
options.E: The trustees are not liable because they acted in good faith and genuinely believed the investment was sound.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The Trustee Act 2000, s 1 imposes a statutory duty of care requiring trustees to exercise such care and skill as is reasonable. Section 4 requires regard to the standard investment criteria, including the need for diversification. Section 5 requires proper investment advice. Concentrating the entire fund in one company without advice breaches all three. E: good faith is not a defence to breach of statutory duty. A: commercial judgment is relevant but within the framework of the TA 2000. D: knowledge of future losses is not required — the breach lies in the failure to diversify and take advice. B: lay trustees still owe a duty of reasonable care; a lay person with no special skills still has a baseline duty.

questions.scenario: A trust has three trustees: A, B, and C. Trustee A, without consulting B and C, sells a significant asset of the trust to a third party at below market value.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: B and C are not liable because they did not participate in the transaction.
options.B: The sale is void because all three trustees must sign any deed.
options.C: The sale may be voidable — trustees must act unanimously, and A's unilateral sale without consulting B and C is a breach of trust for which the liable trustees are jointly and severally liable.
options.D: The sale is valid because A is a trustee with full legal powers over the trust property.
options.E: The sale is valid because A holds legal title jointly with B and C and any one of them may act alone.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Trustees hold legal title jointly and must act unanimously (Luke v South Kensington Hotel Co [1879]). A's unilateral sale is a breach of trust. The sale may be voidable against the third party (unless they are a bona fide purchaser without notice). A, B, and C may all be liable: A for the breach itself; B and C if they failed in their duty to actively supervise trust administration. D: full legal powers must be exercised by all trustees acting together. E: joint legal title does not mean any one trustee can act unilaterally. B: not all transactions require a deed — the issue is unanimity, not formality. A: B and C may be liable for passive acquiescence in or failure to prevent the breach.

questions.scenario: A trustee of a family trust, which owns a valuable painting, purchases the painting from the trust for himself at the current market value, supported by an independent professional valuation.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: The purchase is valid because trustees are entitled to purchase trust assets at arm's length.
options.B: The purchase is voidable only if the beneficiaries can show they received less than market value.
options.C: The purchase is valid because the trustee paid a fair market price supported by an independent valuation.
options.D: The purchase is voidable at the beneficiaries' option — the self-dealing rule prohibits a trustee from purchasing trust property regardless of whether a fair price was paid or good faith was shown.
options.E: The purchase is valid because the trustee obtained an independent valuation, showing good faith.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The self-dealing rule (Ex parte Lacey [1802]; Holder v Holder [1968]) is strict: a trustee may not purchase trust property, whether at market value or otherwise, without the fully informed consent of all beneficiaries or court approval. The transaction is voidable at the beneficiaries' election, regardless of the price paid or good faith shown. C: market value is irrelevant — the rule is prophylactic. A: arm's length dealing does not override the self-dealing rule. E: good faith and independent valuation do not save the transaction without beneficiary consent. B: the beneficiaries need not show they received less than market value — voidability exists regardless of price.

questions.scenario: A trust was created for A for life, remainder to B absolutely. A and B are both adults of full mental capacity and together hold the entire beneficial interest in the trust property. They jointly wish to terminate the trust immediately and call for the property.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether A and B can terminate the trust?

options.A: A and B cannot terminate the trust because trusts are permanent once created.
options.B: Only the settlor can terminate the trust — once the trust is constituted, it belongs to the beneficiaries; the settlor retains no power to revoke or collapse it unless a power of revocation was expressly reserved.
options.C: A and B can terminate the trust — under Saunders v Vautier, where all beneficiaries are adult, of full capacity, and together hold the entire beneficial interest, they may collapse the trust.
options.D: B cannot terminate the trust because his interest is only a remainder and has not vested in possession.
options.E: A and B can only terminate the trust with a court order — the court has inherent jurisdiction to vary or terminate trusts, but the beneficiaries have no unilateral right to demand the property while the trust subsists.

questions.explanation: C is correct. In Saunders v Vautier (1841), the court held that where all beneficiaries are adult, of sound mind, and together hold the entire beneficial interest, they can collectively terminate the trust and call for the property — even if the trust instrument contemplates a longer duration. A holds a vested life interest; B holds a vested interest in remainder. Together they hold the entire beneficial interest and can exercise this right. A: trusts are not permanent — the Saunders v Vautier rule exists precisely to allow termination. B: the settlor has no right to terminate once the trust is constituted, absent a reserved power. E: no court order is required. D: B's interest is vested in interest (though not yet in possession) — it is a present right to future enjoyment, which qualifies for Saunders v Vautier.

questions.scenario: A trust has two trustees, T1 and T2. T1 persuades T2 to agree to an unauthorised investment which loses £100,000. T1 was the driving force and deceived T2 about the nature of the investment.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the liability of T1 and T2?

options.A: T2 is not liable because she relied on T1's expertise — a trustee who acts on the judgment of a more experienced co-trustee in good faith is not in breach, provided she took reasonable steps to satisfy herself the investment was appropriate.
options.B: T1 and T2 are jointly and severally liable for the full £100,000 — all participating trustees are jointly and severally liable, though T2 may seek contribution from T1.
options.C: Only T1 is liable because T2 merely agreed; liability depends on who was the driving force.
options.D: T1 and T2 are each liable for 50% of the loss — since they participated equally in the decision, it would be equitable to divide liability equally between them rather than make either jointly and severally liable for the full sum.
options.E: T2 has a complete defence because T1 deceived her into agreeing — where a trustee has been misled by a co-trustee, equity will not hold the innocent trustee liable for a breach they could not have prevented.

questions.explanation: B is correct. All trustees who participate in or concur with a breach of trust are jointly and severally liable for the full loss (Bahin v Hughes [1886]). T2 agreed to the unauthorised investment — this constitutes participation. Joint and several liability means the beneficiaries can recover the full amount from either trustee. However, as between T1 and T2, the court may apportion responsibility (Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978) and T2 may recover a contribution from T1, particularly given T1's deception. C: the driving force is relevant to contribution between trustees, not to liability to beneficiaries. E: deception by a co-trustee may give T2 a full indemnity from T1 (per Bahin v Hughes dicta), but does not provide a complete defence to beneficiaries. D: joint and several means the beneficiaries can claim the full £100,000 from either, not 50% each. A: reliance on a co-trustee's expertise does not excuse participation in a breach.

questions.scenario: An adult beneficiary, knowing the full facts, urges the trustee to make a particular investment which turns out to be a loss-making breach of trust. The trustee acts on the beneficiary's instigation.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the trustee's position?

options.A: The trustee has no defence because trustees cannot shelter behind a beneficiary's instruction.
options.B: The trustee is partially liable because the beneficiary's consent does not amount to a full defence.
options.C: The trustee has a complete defence — where the breach was committed at the instigation of a consenting adult beneficiary with full knowledge, that beneficiary cannot sue the trustee under s 62 TA 1925.
options.D: The trustee is fully liable because the duty to the other beneficiaries overrides any consent from one beneficiary.
options.E: The trustee is liable because a trustee must always exercise independent judgment.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under s 62 TA 1925, where a breach of trust was committed at the instigation or request or with the consent of a beneficiary, the court may impound that beneficiary's interest to indemnify the trustee. Where the consenting beneficiary is the only beneficiary (or all beneficiaries consent), the trustee has a complete defence against the consenting beneficiary. B: consent of the instigating beneficiary can be a complete defence as against that beneficiary. A: the law specifically provides a defence where a beneficiary has participated in or instigated the breach. E: independent judgment is expected, but a beneficiary who instigates a breach cannot then hold the trustee liable for following their instigation. D: the duty to other beneficiaries does not affect the defence as against the consenting beneficiary — s 62 TA 1925 impounds that beneficiary's own interest to meet the trustee's liability.

questions.scenario: A trustee manages a charitable trust fund worth £2 million. She invests the entire fund in UK government gilts (regarded as extremely safe), without taking professional investment advice.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the trustee's position under the Trustee Act 2000?

options.A: The trustee may be in breach — under ss 4-5 TA 2000, trustees must consider diversification and take proper investment advice; investing the entire fund in gilts without advice may fail both requirements.
options.B: The trustee has not breached her duty because the decision to invest in gilts was commercially reasonable.
options.C: The trustee has breached her duty because gilts are not listed in the trust instrument as authorised investments.
options.D: The trustee has not breached her duty because gilts are entirely safe and she has a duty to protect the capital.
options.E: The trustee has not breached her duty because she sought proper advice before investing.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Sections 4 and 5 of the Trustee Act 2000 require trustees to have regard to the standard investment criteria — including suitability and the need for diversification — and to take and consider proper advice. Even if gilts are individually safe, investing the entire fund in one class of asset without any advice fails both the diversification requirement and the advice requirement. E: safety of individual investments does not satisfy the diversification or advice requirements. D: commercial reasonableness must be assessed within the statutory framework. C: ss 3-4 TA 2000 give trustees a general power of investment in any assets, not limited to those listed in the trust instrument. B: the facts state she did not take advice — this contradicts option E.

questions.scenario: Pension fund trustees have a discretionary power to award additional benefits to members 'at the trustees' absolute discretion'. A member applies for additional benefits. The trustees adopt a blanket policy of refusing all such applications without considering them individually.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the position?

options.A: The pension regulator must resolve the dispute rather than the courts.
options.B: The trustees are in breach of trust because they must always award additional benefits when asked.
options.C: The trustees' policy is valid because consistent policy-making is generally permissible in trust law.
options.D: The trustees' refusal to consider the application amounts to a failure to exercise their discretion — 'absolute' means unreviewable on the merits, not that discretion need never be exercised at all.
options.E: The trustees are entitled to adopt a blanket policy of refusal because the discretion is 'absolute'.

questions.explanation: D is correct. 'Absolute discretion' in a trust context means the trustees' decision on the merits is not reviewable by the court — the court cannot substitute its own view. However, trustees must still genuinely exercise their discretion. A blanket policy of refusal without individual consideration is a failure to exercise the discretion at all, which is itself a breach of trust (McPhail v Doulton [1971]; Klug v Klug [1918]). E: 'absolute' means unreviewable on the merits, not that the discretion need not be exercised at all. A: there is no obligation to award benefits — only to consider. C: consistent policy-making as a starting point may be permissible, but a blanket refusal to consider is not mere policy. B: the courts do have jurisdiction over breach of trust by pension trustees.

questions.scenario: A trustee holds a commercial lease on trust for a minor beneficiary. When the lease expires, the landlord refuses to renew it for the minor but offers to grant a new lease to the trustee personally. The trustee accepts and operates the premises profitably.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the trustee's liability?

options.A: The trustee is not liable because the landlord refused to grant the lease to the minor, so no opportunity was lost to the trust.
options.B: The trustee is liable only if he acted dishonestly in taking the lease for himself.
options.C: The trustee is liable only if he made a profit greater than the trust would have made.
options.D: The trustee is not liable because the trust could not hold the new lease anyway, as the minor has no legal capacity.
options.E: The trustee is liable for breach of fiduciary duty — in Keech v Sandford, a trustee who takes a personal benefit from an opportunity arising through the trust must hold that benefit on constructive trust for the beneficiary.

questions.explanation: E is correct. In Keech v Sandford (1726), Lord King LC held that a trustee who renewed a lease for their own benefit — even where the landlord refused to renew for the trust — held the renewed lease on constructive trust for the beneficiary. The no-profit rule is strict: the fact that the trust itself could not have taken the lease is no defence. A: the unavailability of the opportunity to the trust is precisely what was considered and rejected in Keech v Sandford. C: profit comparison is irrelevant — the rule is strict. D: the minor's incapacity is irrelevant — the trustee still holds for the benefit of the minor. B: dishonesty is not required — the no-profit rule is strict (Boardman v Phipps [1967]).

questions.scenario: A solicitor acting for trustees of a family trust uses confidential information obtained in his capacity as agent for the trust to identify and purchase shares in a company. He makes a profit of £80,000. He acted in good faith and the trust itself could not have made the same purchase.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the solicitor's liability?

options.A: The solicitor is liable only for the profits made, but he retains the shares he purchased.
options.B: The solicitor is not liable because he acted in good faith and the trust itself could not have made the purchase.
options.C: The solicitor must account for the profits — under Boardman v Phipps, a fiduciary who makes an unauthorised profit by exploiting their position must account for it regardless of good faith.
options.D: The solicitor is not liable because he did not hold legal title to the trust property.
options.E: The solicitor must account for profits only if the beneficiaries can show the trust suffered a loss.

questions.explanation: C is correct, applying Boardman v Phipps [1967]. The House of Lords held (3-2) that a fiduciary who uses information or opportunities obtained through their fiduciary position to make a personal profit must account for that profit, even if they acted in good faith and even if the trust itself could not have made the same gain. The no-profit rule is strict. B: good faith is irrelevant to the strict no-profit rule. E: the solicitor must account for all profits — the court may also impose a constructive trust over the shares themselves. D: legal title to trust property is irrelevant — the solicitor used information obtained as fiduciary. A: no loss to the trust is required — the remedy is account of profits, not compensation for loss.

questions.scenario: A trustee wishes to purchase trust property for himself. He discloses the transaction fully to all three adult beneficiaries, who all have full legal capacity and full knowledge of the facts, and all three consent in writing.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the transaction is valid?

options.A: The transaction is voidable because the consent of beneficiaries can always be withdrawn.
options.B: The transaction is valid only if a court approves it in advance.
options.C: The transaction is valid — fully informed consent of all adult beneficiaries with full legal capacity is a recognised exception to the self-dealing rule.
options.D: The transaction is only valid if the trustee pays above market value.
options.E: The transaction is void regardless of consent because the self-dealing rule is absolute.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The self-dealing rule can be overridden by the fully informed consent of all beneficiaries who are of full age and capacity and who have full knowledge of the material facts (Holder v Holder [1968]; Saunders v Vautier principles). Here all three adult beneficiaries consented with full knowledge — this is a recognised exception. A: consent given with full knowledge cannot be freely withdrawn — this overstates the position. B: court approval is an alternative route where beneficiaries lack capacity; it is not required where all adult beneficiaries give fully informed consent. D: price is not the determining factor — the rule turns on fully informed consent, not market value. E: the self-dealing rule is not absolute — it can be overridden by the fully informed consent of all adult beneficiaries with full capacity.

questions.scenario: A solicitor assists a trustee in setting up a scheme to transfer trust assets to offshore accounts in breach of trust. The solicitor charges ordinary professional fees for this work. He is aware that the transactions are unusual but does not inquire further.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the solicitor's liability?

options.A: The solicitor is liable only for the fees he received, not for all trust losses.
options.B: The solicitor is liable only if he knew the transaction was a breach of trust.
options.C: The solicitor has no liability because he did not personally receive trust property.
options.D: The solicitor is not liable because he was acting on the instructions of the trustee.
options.E: The solicitor is liable for dishonest assistance — the test is whether his conduct was dishonest by the standard of ordinary honest people, assessed objectively.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Dishonest assistance (Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995]) makes a stranger to a trust liable if they assist a breach of trust dishonestly. Dishonesty is assessed objectively: given the solicitor's actual knowledge of the unusual nature of the transactions, would an ordinary honest person in his position have proceeded without inquiry? If so, liability arises. C: receipt of trust property is not required for dishonest assistance — it is the assistance that triggers liability. A: if liable for dishonest assistance, the solicitor is liable for the full loss caused, not merely the fees. D: following instructions is no defence where those instructions are dishonestly assisted. B: actual knowledge of the breach is not required — objective dishonesty (assessed by reference to the defendant's actual knowledge and beliefs) is the test.

questions.scenario: A trustee transfers trust money to a third party recipient in breach of trust. The recipient initially receives the money in good faith but subsequently discovers the money was transferred in breach of trust. He uses the money to purchase a car.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the recipient's liability?

options.A: The recipient is not liable because they have already spent the money on the car.
options.B: The recipient is liable only for the difference between the value of the money received and the value of the car.
options.C: The recipient has no liability because they received the money in good faith and without notice.
options.D: The recipient is only liable if they actively knew at the time of receipt that there was a breach.
options.E: The recipient is liable for knowing receipt — once a person receives trust property and subsequently discovers the breach, it would be unconscionable to retain the benefit and they must account for its value.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Knowing receipt requires: (1) receipt of trust property in breach of trust; (2) knowledge (actual or constructive) of the breach; and (3) that it would be unconscionable to retain the property (BCCI v Akindele [2001]). Knowledge acquired after receipt but before spending can suffice — once the recipient knows of the breach, it is unconscionable to use the money. C: good faith at the time of receipt is not a complete defence if knowledge is later acquired. D: knowledge need not exist at the moment of receipt — later knowledge can trigger liability. A: spending the money after acquiring knowledge of the breach does not extinguish liability — the recipient is personally liable to account. B: the recipient is liable for the full value of the trust property received, not merely the difference.

questions.scenario: A company director uses confidential information belonging to his company to purchase shares in a target company before a takeover announcement, making a profit of £150,000. He used his own money to buy the shares.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the remedy available to the company?

options.A: The company can only claim if it can show the director prevented it from making the same profit.
options.B: The company can claim equitable compensation for the loss caused by the director's breach of duty.
options.C: The director can keep the shares but must pay compensation equal to any loss suffered by the company.
options.D: The company has no claim because the director used his own money to buy the shares.
options.E: The company can require the director to account for his profits — a fiduciary who exploits confidential information obtained through their position must account for any profits made, regardless of whether the company suffered a loss.

questions.explanation: E is correct. A company director is a fiduciary (Companies Act 2006, ss 175-177). The no-profit rule requires a fiduciary who makes an unauthorised profit through exploitation of their fiduciary position to account for that profit (Regal (Hastings) v Gulliver [1942]; Boardman v Phipps [1967]). No loss to the company is required — the remedy is an account of profits. B: equitable compensation requires loss; the no-profit rule is a profit-stripping remedy, not a loss-based claim. D: using personal money is irrelevant — the profit was made through a fiduciary position. A: preventing the company from making the profit is not required. C: the director cannot keep the profit — they must account for it.

questions.scenario: A settlor transfers £100,000 to trustees to hold on trust for 'such of my friends as the trustees shall select'. The trust fails for uncertainty of objects. No alternative destination for the money was specified.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes what happens to the money?

options.A: The money is held on constructive trust for the intended beneficiaries.
options.B: The money passes to the Crown as bona vacantia because the trust has failed.
options.C: The trustees may retain the money as they hold legal title.
options.D: The money is held on automatic resulting trust for the settlor — where an express trust fails, the beneficial interest results back to the settlor.
options.E: The money passes to the beneficiaries who were named as alternative objects.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Where an express trust fails wholly or in part — whether for uncertainty, illegality, or other reasons — the beneficial interest does not remain with the trustees or pass to the Crown, but results back to the settlor by way of automatic resulting trust (Westdeutsche Landesbank v Islington LBC [1996]). B: bona vacantia applies where there is no other equitable owner — a resulting trust to the settlor takes priority. C: trustees hold legal title but not beneficially — they are obliged to return the property. E: no alternative objects were named — there is no basis for passing to anyone else. A: a constructive trust requires additional grounds; the correct analysis is automatic resulting trust.

questions.scenario: A transfers £20,000 into a bank account in B's name without receiving any payment or other consideration. There is no express declaration of any trust. A claims the money should be returned.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: B holds the money on constructive trust because A transferred it by mistake — where property is transferred under a fundamental mistake, the recipient is unjustly enriched and equity imposes a constructive trust to reverse the enrichment.
options.B: A has no claim because the transfer was voluntary and irrevocable — once A chose to pay money to B without reservation or condition, the transfer was complete and A cannot subsequently seek to recover it in the absence of fraud or mistake.
options.C: There is a presumption of advancement because A transferred the money voluntarily — a voluntary transfer in the absence of consideration raises a presumption that the transferor intended to make a gift, rebutting any resulting trust.
options.D: There is a presumption of resulting trust — equity presumes the transferee holds on trust for the transferor where property is transferred voluntarily without consideration, rebuttable by evidence of gift intention.
options.E: B owns the money outright because A made a voluntary transfer — once A chose to pay money into B's account without reservation, the money became B's property and A retains no legal or equitable claim over it.

questions.explanation: D is correct. In Dyer v Dyer (1788), the court confirmed the presumption of resulting trust: where property is transferred voluntarily and without consideration, equity presumes the transferee holds on resulting trust for the transferor. The presumption is rebuttable — B can show A intended a gift. B: voluntary transfer does not automatically transfer beneficial ownership — equity looks at intention. C: the presumption of advancement applies in specific relationships (parent-child, husband-wife) not to stranger transfers. A: a constructive trust requires grounds such as fraud or unjust enrichment — not applicable on these facts. E: the resulting trust analysis provides A with a claim.

questions.scenario: A father transfers a house into his adult daughter's name to help her get on the housing ladder. No consideration is paid and there is no express declaration of trust.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: There is no presumption of advancement in transfers to adult children.
options.B: The presumption of advancement applies — where a father transfers property to a child, equity presumes an outright gift, rebuttable by evidence of contrary intention.
options.C: The presumption of advancement was abolished by the Equality Act 2010.
options.D: The house is held on constructive trust because the daughter benefited from the transfer.
options.E: The house is held on resulting trust because there was no consideration.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The presumption of advancement applies to transfers from a parent to a child (Dyer v Dyer [1788]; Warren v Gurney [1944]). This applies to adult children as well as minors — the relationship, not the age, is determinative. The presumption can be rebutted by evidence of contrary intention. E: the presumption of resulting trust is displaced by the presumption of advancement in this relationship. D: incorrect — the presumption of advancement applies regardless of the child's age. A: there are insufficient facts for a constructive trust — the presumption of advancement is the applicable analysis. C: the Equality Act 2010 s 199 was enacted but has not been brought into force; the presumption of advancement remains operative in English law.

questions.scenario: A couple purchase a house in the man's sole name. The woman contributes half the purchase price from her own savings. There is no express declaration of their respective beneficial shares.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the woman's position?

options.A: The woman has a resulting trust interest proportionate to her financial contribution — she contributed half the purchase price so holds a 50% resulting trust interest.
options.B: The woman has no interest unless she can show an express oral agreement that she would have a share.
options.C: The woman may have a common intention constructive trust — her financial contribution evidences common intention, and the court will assess the extent of her share by reference to the whole course of dealing between the parties.
options.D: The woman is entitled to the full value of the house as the man has been unjustly enriched.
options.E: The woman has no interest because the house is in the man's sole name.

questions.explanation: C is correct. In Stack v Dowden [2007] and Jones v Kernott [2011], the Supreme Court confirmed that in shared home disputes, the primary analysis is the common intention constructive trust. A financial contribution to the purchase price is strong evidence of common intention, but the court assesses the extent of the share by reference to the whole course of dealing between the parties. A: the resulting trust analysis (strict financial contribution) was downplayed in Stack v Dowden for family home cases. B: financial contribution to the purchase creates an equitable interest even without legal title. D: an express oral agreement is not required — financial conduct can establish common intention. E: unjust enrichment is not the basis of the claim; it is common intention constructive trust.

questions.scenario: An unmarried couple buy a house in joint names with no declaration of their respective beneficial shares. The woman gives up her career to raise their children. The man pays the mortgage exclusively. They separate and dispute ownership.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes how the court will approach the question of shares?

options.A: Because the house is in joint names, the man and woman hold 50/50 in equity regardless of respective contributions.
options.B: The woman is entitled to a larger share because she sacrificed her career.
options.C: The man is entitled to a larger share because he paid the mortgage exclusively.
options.D: The starting point is equal shares because the property is in joint names, but this can be displaced if the whole course of dealing between the parties shows a different common intention.
options.E: The court applies the resulting trust analysis and divides shares strictly according to financial contributions.

questions.explanation: D is correct. In Stack v Dowden [2007], Baroness Hale confirmed that where property is in joint names, the starting point in equity is equal (50/50) shares — equity follows the law. However, the presumption can be rebutted if the whole course of dealing between the parties — including non-financial contributions such as childcare and career sacrifice — shows a different common intention. A: the presumption of equal shares applies in joint names cases but is rebuttable — the starting point is 50/50, not an irrebuttable equal division. B: career sacrifice is a relevant factor in assessing common intention, but no single factor is determinative — the court looks at the whole course of dealing. C: mortgage payments are relevant but not automatically decisive — exclusive payment does not override the joint names presumption without more. E: the resulting trust approach (strict financial contributions) was explicitly rejected for family home cases in Stack v Dowden.

questions.scenario: A couple (A and B) purchase a house in joint names. A contributes 65% of the purchase price from her own savings and B contributes 35%. There is no express declaration of their beneficial shares. They later dispute ownership.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the approach the court will take?

options.A: A holds 65% because she is entitled to the full value of her financial contribution.
options.B: Under the resulting trust analysis, A holds 65% and B holds 35% in equity.
options.C: The court applies the resulting trust analysis first and then considers whether the CICT alters the result.
options.D: The starting point is equal (50/50) shares because the property is in joint names, but this can be displaced if the whole course of dealing — including non-financial contributions — shows a different common intention.
options.E: Because the house is in joint names, both A and B hold 50/50 in equity regardless of contributions and this cannot be displaced.

questions.explanation: D is correct. In Stack v Dowden [2007], Baroness Hale confirmed that where property is in joint names, equity follows the law — the starting presumption is 50/50. This can be displaced only in unusual cases where the whole course of dealing shows a different common intention. A's 65% contribution is relevant but not automatically determinative. A: the resulting trust approach (strict financial contributions) was explicitly not preferred for family home cases in Stack v Dowden. E: 50/50 is a starting presumption, not an immutable rule. B: the resulting trust analysis does not apply — common intention is the framework. C: the CICT is the primary framework; resulting trust analysis is not applied first.

questions.scenario: A solicitor is instructed by a client to find suitable commercial premises for the client's business. During the search, the solicitor identifies a property which he considers ideal but purchases it himself without disclosing this to the client.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the solicitor's position?

options.A: The solicitor holds the property on resulting trust for the client because the client has a beneficial interest.
options.B: The solicitor is free to purchase the property because he used his own money.
options.C: The solicitor is only liable if the client can prove the property would have been profitable for the client.
options.D: The solicitor is not in breach of duty because the property was not in the client's existing portfolio.
options.E: The solicitor holds the property on constructive trust for the client — as a fiduciary, a solicitor cannot take for themselves an opportunity that arises within the scope of their instructions and falls within their duty.

questions.explanation: E is correct. A solicitor is a fiduciary in relation to their client. The no-conflict and no-profit rules prevent a fiduciary from exploiting an opportunity that arises within the scope of their fiduciary duty — here, finding suitable premises for the client. The solicitor must hold the property on constructive trust for the client (Boardman v Phipps [1967]; CMS Dolphin v Simonet [2002]). B: using personal funds is irrelevant — the breach lies in taking the opportunity. A: the opportunity arose precisely because the solicitor was acting for the client. D: a resulting trust requires different facts — the constructive trust arising from breach of fiduciary duty is the correct analysis. C: loss to the client is not required — the remedy is account of profits or constructive trust.

questions.scenario: A trustee makes an unauthorised investment in high-risk bonds. The investment falls in value, causing a loss to the trust fund of £50,000. The trustee argues that even an authorised investment in similar bonds would have fallen by £20,000 in any event.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the trustee's liability?

options.A: The trustee is liable for the full loss of £50,000 — equitable compensation requires the trustee to restore the trust fund to the position it would have been in had the breach not occurred.
options.B: The trustee is liable only for the loss caused by the specific breach, assessed on a 'but for' basis as in common law negligence; liability is therefore limited to £30,000.
options.C: The trustee's liability is reduced proportionately to reflect the losses that would have occurred in any event.
options.D: The trustee is not liable if he acted in good faith when making the investment.
options.E: The trustee is only liable if the beneficiaries can prove the authorised investment would have been profitable.

questions.explanation: A is correct. In Target Holdings v Redferns [1996] and AIB Group (UK) v Mark Redler & Co [2014], the courts confirmed that equitable compensation for breach of trust requires the trustee to restore the trust fund to the position it would have been in but for the breach. While causation remains relevant, the court is not confined to a strict 'but for' analysis of counterfactual loss. The full £50,000 loss attributable to the breach must be restored. B: the strict common law 'but for' test is not applied in the same way. D: good faith does not excuse an unauthorised investment. C: proportionate reduction for counterfactual loss is not a general principle of equitable compensation. E: the beneficiaries do not need to prove profitability of the authorised alternative.

questions.scenario: A trustee, who has no professional expertise in investment, delegates investment decisions to a professional stockbroker without reviewing the stockbroker's actions. The stockbroker makes unsuitable investments, causing loss. The trustee seeks relief under s 61 TA 1925, arguing he acted in good faith.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the court is likely to grant relief?

options.A: The court will grant relief because the trustee genuinely believed the stockbroker was competent.
options.B: The court will grant full relief because the breach was caused by the stockbroker's misconduct, not the trustee's.
options.C: The court is unlikely to grant relief — under s 61 TA 1925, the trustee must have acted both honestly and reasonably; failing to monitor the stockbroker's performance is not reasonable conduct.
options.D: The court will grant relief because delegation to a professional agent is always permissible.
options.E: The court must grant relief because the trustee is a lay person and cannot be held to a professional standard.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Section 61 TA 1925 requires the trustee to have acted both honestly and reasonably. Delegation to a stockbroker may be reasonable in principle (s 11 TA 2000 permits delegation of investment management), but the trustee must also monitor and review the delegate's performance (s 22 TA 2000). Failing to do so is not reasonable. A: genuine belief in competence is not the same as reasonable conduct. D: delegation is permissible but subject to monitoring requirements. E: lay status reduces but does not eliminate the duty of care — reasonableness is still required. B: the trustee's failure to monitor is a separate and concurrent breach.

questions.scenario: A trustee misappropriates £50,000 of trust money into her personal bank account, which already contained £10,000. She withdraws £55,000 (dissipated), reducing the balance to £5,000. She then receives a salary of £30,000, bringing the balance to £35,000. The beneficiaries seek to trace.
questions.stem: What is the maximum sum the beneficiaries can trace into the current account balance of £35,000?

options.A: £30,000 — the amount of the last salary deposit, because the salary payment replenished the account and the beneficiary can trace into all funds subsequently received by the trustee into the mixed account, not just the original trust money.
options.B: Nothing — the trust money was fully dissipated when the account fell below zero, and once the mixed fund is exhausted the proprietary tracing claim is extinguished; the beneficiary is left only with a personal claim against the trustee.
options.C: £5,000 — the lowest intermediate balance rule caps the claim at the lowest point the account reached after trust money was paid in; subsequent deposits of the trustee's own money cannot revive the claim.
options.D: £50,000 — the full amount of trust money misappropriated, because the beneficiary's proprietary claim attaches to all the trust money paid into the account and is not reduced by the trustee's subsequent dissipation of the fund.
options.E: £35,000 — the full current balance, since trust money was mixed with personal funds.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The lowest intermediate balance rule (James Roscoe (Bolton) Ltd v Winder [1915]) provides that where a mixed fund falls below the amount of trust money paid in, the trust's claim is capped at the lowest intermediate balance — here £5,000. The subsequent salary payment of £30,000 belongs to the trustee personally and cannot revive or increase the trust's proprietary claim. B: the trust's claim is capped at the lowest intermediate balance, not the original deposit. D: the current balance includes £30,000 of the trustee's own salary — the trust can only claim up to the lowest intermediate balance. A: the salary deposit is the trustee's personal money and cannot be claimed by the trust. E: £5,000 of trust money remained even after the dissipation.

questions.scenario: A trustee wrongly mixes trust money of £30,000 with £30,000 belonging to an innocent third party (who had no knowledge of the trust). The trustee withdraws £30,000 and dissipates it. £30,000 remains in the account.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes how the remaining £30,000 is divided between the trust and the innocent third party?

options.A: The trust takes priority under the rule in Re Hallett's Estate and takes £30,000 first.
options.B: The £30,000 belongs to the trustee who must pay each party separately.
options.C: The £30,000 is divided equally between the trust and the innocent third party — where two innocent parties suffer from a trustee's wrongdoing in a mixed fund, the remaining assets are shared proportionately (pari passu).
options.D: The trust takes the entire £30,000 because its claim has priority over private individuals.
options.E: The innocent third party takes the entire £30,000 because the trustee's wrongdoing should not be allowed to harm innocent parties.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Where trust money is mixed with the money of an innocent third party (not the wrongdoing trustee), both parties are equally innocent — the loss must be shared proportionately (Re Diplock [1948]; Barlow Clowes v Vaughan [1992]). Each contributed £30,000 to the £60,000 mixed fund; each suffers 50% of the £30,000 remaining. A: there is no priority rule for the trust over an innocent third party. D: the trust is equally innocent — both must share the loss equally. B: the trustee has no claim to the remaining £30,000 as they caused the loss. E: Re Hallett applies to mixtures of trust money with the trustee's own money — not to mixtures with innocent third parties.

questions.scenario: A trustee draws a cheque for £30,000 on the trust account, which is then overdrawn, and uses the proceeds to purchase a painting which has since increased in value to £80,000.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the beneficiaries can assert a proprietary claim over the painting?

options.A: The beneficiaries can trace into the painting — under Re Oatway, where applying the Hallett presumption would leave the beneficiaries worse off, they may instead claim a proprietary interest in assets purchased from the mixed fund.
options.B: The beneficiaries can only assert a personal claim against the trustee for £30,000.
options.C: The beneficiaries cannot claim the painting because it is the trustee's personal property.
options.D: The beneficiaries cannot trace into the painting because the account was overdrawn at the time of the purchase, breaking the chain of tracing.
options.E: The beneficiaries can trace into the salary funds deposited after the overdraft.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The traditional rule was that tracing into an overdrawn account was impossible (Re Diplock [1948]). However, in Foskett v McKeown [2001] and later developed in Federal Republic of Brazil v Durant International Corporation [2015] (PC), the court recognised 'backwards tracing': where there is a coordinated transaction between a debit from the trust account and the acquisition of an asset (here, purchasing the painting using an overdraft on the trust account), the court may treat the asset as purchased with trust funds. D: backward tracing addresses the overdrawn account problem. B: a proprietary claim against the painting is available and preferable to a personal claim. C: tracing into subsequent salary deposits is the wrong analysis here — the painting is the traceable proceed. E: the painting was acquired with trust funds — it is subject to a proprietary claim.

questions.scenario: A trustee misappropriates £30,000 of trust money and pays it into his personal bank account which already contained £20,000. He then withdraws £10,000 (dissipated on a holiday), leaving £40,000. He subsequently withdraws £25,000 to buy shares, leaving £15,000 in the account.
questions.stem: Applying Re Hallett's Estate and Re Oatway, which of the following best describes the beneficiaries' options?

options.A: The beneficiaries must apply Re Hallett and can only claim the remaining account balance after the trustee's money has been spent first.
options.B: The beneficiaries can claim only the £15,000 remaining in the account.
options.C: The beneficiaries may choose to claim either the £15,000 in the account (under Re Hallett, treating early withdrawals as the trustee's own money) or the shares (under Re Oatway, if that produces a better outcome).
options.D: The beneficiaries cannot trace because the trust money was mixed with the trustee's own funds.
options.E: The beneficiaries can claim both the £15,000 and the shares simultaneously.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Re Hallett's Estate [1880] presumes the trustee spent their own money first — treating the first £20,000 withdrawn as the trustee's money and the second £10,000 dissipated as reducing the trustee's portion. Under Hallett, the shares purchase could be treated as the trustee's money, and the £15,000 balance would be trust money. Re Oatway [1903] allows the beneficiaries to depart from the Hallett presumption if it produces a worse outcome — they may instead claim the shares were purchased with trust money, then claim the remaining balance as trust money too (if the maths works). The beneficiaries can choose whichever analysis is most beneficial. E: this is too limited — Re Oatway may allow the beneficiaries to claim the shares. B: this is Re Hallett alone and ignores Re Oatway. D: mixing does not defeat equitable tracing — it is precisely the scenario equitable tracing addresses. A: the beneficiaries cannot claim both simultaneously — they must choose.

questions.scenario: A wealthy businesswoman says to her brother: 'I want you to look after some money for my children. I've put £200,000 into an account in your name. Use your judgment — just make sure the children benefit.' No further documentation is prepared.
