questions.stem: Which of the following best analyses the trust law issues on these facts?

options.A: A valid trust was created but only for 50% of the fund because the businesswoman is also an object.
options.B: A trust was created but it fails because there is no trustee willing to act.
options.C: There are potential issues with certainty of intention — 'want you to look after' and 'use your judgment' are ambiguous and may be precatory; subject matter (£200,000) and objects (children) appear certain.
options.D: No trust was created because the money was transferred to the brother as legal owner.
options.E: A valid trust was created: the intention is clear, the subject matter (£200,000) is certain, and the objects (children) are identifiable.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The words used — 'want you to look after' and 'use your judgment' — are potentially precatory or equivocal. They do not clearly impose a legally binding obligation on the brother, as opposed to expressing a wish or conferring a discretion without making him a trustee. This creates real doubt about certainty of intention. The subject matter (£200,000) is certain, and the objects (the businesswoman's children) are identifiable. But without certainty of intention, no trust arises. E: intention is not clearly established on these facts. A: the businesswoman is not stated to be an object. D: transfer of legal ownership does not preclude a trust, but the intention analysis remains uncertain. B: the brother is the named quasi-trustee and willing to act.

questions.scenario: A father lent his son £1,500. The son subsequently moved into the father's house as a lodger and it was agreed that the son would pay no rent in lieu of repaying the loan. The father never asked for the money back and never enforced the agreement. When the father died, the son was appointed as his administrator.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the legal position regarding the £1,500 debt?

options.A: The debt is owed because Strong v Bird only applies to gifts of property, not releases of debt.
options.B: The debt is owed because the agreement to set off was contractually binding.
options.C: The debt is still owed because the father never formally released it.
options.D: The debt was released by the father's conduct in not seeking rent payments.
options.E: Under Strong v Bird [1874], the debt was extinguished when the son became administrator — a continuing donative intention is perfected on the donee's appointment as executor or administrator.

questions.explanation: E is correct. In Strong v Bird (1874), the court held that where: (1) there is a present and continuing intention to release a debt (or make a gift); and (2) the donee/releasee is appointed executor or administrator of the donor's estate, the release or gift is perfected on appointment. Here, the father had a continuing intention to forgive the debt (evidenced by the rent arrangement and never seeking repayment). When the son became administrator, the debt was extinguished. C: the intention to release was continuous and was perfected on the son's appointment. D: while conduct may be evidence of intention, the legal mechanism is Strong v Bird. B: contractual analysis is incorrect — the arrangement is better analysed under Strong v Bird. A: Strong v Bird applies to both gifts of property and releases of debts (Re Freeland [1952]).

questions.scenario: A testator's will leaves the entire estate to his solicitor 'absolutely'. Before executing the will, the testator tells the solicitor that he wishes the property to be held for his mistress and communicates the terms of the intended trust. The solicitor nods in acknowledgment. The testator dies and the solicitor claims the property absolutely.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether a fully secret trust arises?

options.A: A constructive trust arises automatically because the solicitor has been unjustly enriched.
options.B: No trust arises because the solicitor did not expressly agree to hold the property on trust.
options.C: A trust arises but it is invalid because it was not declared in the will.
options.D: No trust arises because the will does not mention the mistress.
options.E: A fully secret trust arises — the requirements are a clear intention to create a trust, communication of the terms to the trustee before death, and acceptance (express or implied) by the trustee.

questions.explanation: E is correct. A fully secret trust (FST) arises outside the will — the will need not mention it. The requirements are: (1) intention to create a trust (communicated by the testator); (2) communication of the terms to the intended trustee during the testator's lifetime; and (3) acceptance by the trustee, which may be express or implied (a nod suffices: Ottaway v Norman [1972]). The solicitor's nod constitutes implied acceptance and imposes the trust. B: express agreement is not required — implied acceptance suffices. C: an FST is by definition absent from the face of the will. D: unjust enrichment is not the basis — it is the equitable doctrine of secret trusts. A: FSTs operate outside the will precisely because equity does not want the Wills Act to be used as an instrument of fraud.

questions.scenario: A testator's will leaves property 'to my executor John on trust for purposes I have communicated to him'. Before executing the will, John has not received any communication of the trust's terms. After execution, the testator writes to John setting out the purposes.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the position regarding half-secret trusts?

options.A: John may follow the later communication because the testator's later instructions represent a valid codicil.
options.B: John can follow the later communication because it represents the testator's final wishes.
options.C: For a half-secret trust, communication must occur before or at the time the will is executed — communication after execution is too late, and the trustee holds on resulting trust for the estate.
options.D: The trust is invalid because half-secret trusts cannot be created.
options.E: The trust fails because the terms were communicated after the will was executed.

questions.explanation: C is correct. For half-secret trusts (where the will reveals the existence of a trust but not its terms), communication must take place before or at the time the will is executed (Re Keen [1937]). Communication after execution cannot be incorporated into a trust already declared on the face of the will. The will says the terms have already been communicated — a later communication contradicts the will and cannot take effect. B: the testator's later wishes cannot override the rule in Re Keen. E: partly correct — but the consequence is not simply failure; John holds on resulting trust for the estate. D: overstated — half-secret trusts are recognised but subject to strict rules. A: a letter cannot be a codicil — a codicil requires compliance with the Wills Act 1837.

questions.scenario: A company (Barclays) lends money to another company (Rolls Razor) for the specific purpose of paying a dividend to shareholders. The money is paid into a designated account. Before the dividend is paid, Rolls Razor is placed in liquidation.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the legal analysis?

options.A: The money passes to the Crown as bona vacantia because the purpose failed.
options.B: The money is held on a Quistclose trust — where money is lent for a specific purpose that fails, a resulting trust arises in favour of the lender and the money does not form part of the borrower's general assets.
options.C: The shareholders are entitled to the money because it was earmarked for their dividend.
options.D: Barclays can recover the money as a secured creditor because the loan was secured on the account.
options.E: The money forms part of Rolls Razor's general assets on insolvency because it was a loan and the lender ranks as an unsecured creditor.

questions.explanation: B is correct. In Barclays Bank v Quistclose Investments Ltd [1970], the House of Lords held that where money is lent for a specific purpose, with mutual intention that it cannot be used for any other purpose, the money is held on primary trust for that purpose. When the purpose fails (the dividend could not be paid), the money is held on secondary resulting trust for the lender. Barclays had notice and could not set off the sum against Rolls Razor's overdraft. E: the Quistclose trust analysis prevents the money being treated as part of general assets. D: the recovery is proprietary via trust, not contractual security. C: the shareholders had no proprietary interest — the purpose trust was for their benefit but the secondary trust on failure was for Barclays. A: bona vacantia does not apply — the resulting trust in favour of Barclays takes priority.

questions.scenario: The beneficiaries of an existing family trust wish to vary its terms to reduce inheritance tax. Two adult beneficiaries consent but there are minor beneficiaries and unborn future beneficiaries who cannot consent.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes how the variation might be achieved?

options.A: Only the settlor can vary the trust terms; the beneficiaries have no right to do so.
options.B: The court can approve the variation under the Variation of Trusts Act 1958 on behalf of minor and unborn beneficiaries, provided the variation is for their benefit.
options.C: The variation can be made under Saunders v Vautier if the consenting adult beneficiaries together hold the entire beneficial interest.
options.D: The Variation of Trusts Act 1958 allows the court to approve an arrangement on behalf of those who cannot consent — minors, unborn beneficiaries, and those lacking capacity — provided it is for their benefit.
options.E: The variation is automatically valid because the purpose is tax reduction, which benefits all beneficiaries.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under the Variation of Trusts Act 1958, the court has jurisdiction to approve an arrangement on behalf of: (a) minors; (b) persons yet unborn; (c) those lacking capacity; and (d) persons with discretionary interests under protective trusts (s 1(1)). Court approval on behalf of those who cannot consent, combined with the consent of those who can, achieves a binding variation. B: Saunders v Vautier requires all beneficiaries to consent — here there are minor and unborn beneficiaries who cannot, so Saunders v Vautier is inapplicable unless all interests are held by adults. A: partially correct but incomplete — both adult consent and court approval are required. E: the purpose of the variation does not make it automatically valid — a court order is needed on behalf of those who cannot consent. C: beneficiaries have rights under VTA 1958 and Saunders v Vautier — the settlor has no general post-creation power of variation.

questions.scenario: A trustee manages an investment trust. In breach of fiduciary duty, she uses confidential information from the trust to make personal investments, investing £50,000 of her own money and making a profit of £150,000. The trust itself suffers no financial loss.
questions.stem: Which remedy is most advantageous for the beneficiaries and most likely to be granted?

options.A: An account of profits — a fiduciary who makes an unauthorised profit through their position must account for it; the beneficiaries can claim the full £150,000 regardless of any loss to the trust.
options.B: Equitable compensation of £50,000 — the amount the trustee invested.
options.C: Common law damages of £150,000 — the profit made.
options.D: Rescission of the investment — the trustee must unwind the investment and return the money to her personal funds.
options.E: Equitable compensation is unavailable because the trust suffered no loss; the beneficiaries have no remedy.

questions.explanation: A is correct. An account of profits is available where a fiduciary has made an unauthorised profit through exploitation of their fiduciary position (Boardman v Phipps [1967]; Regal (Hastings) v Gulliver [1942]). No loss to the trust is required. The beneficiaries can elect for an account of profits (£150,000) or claim a constructive trust over the investments themselves (which may produce an even greater recovery if the investments continue to increase). E: equitable compensation is a loss-based remedy — inappropriate where the trust suffered no loss. B: incorrect — the account of profits remedy is available regardless of loss. C: common law damages require loss — they are not the appropriate equitable remedy for breach of fiduciary duty. D: rescission would unwind the transaction and deprive the beneficiaries of the profit — not the most advantageous remedy.

questions.scenario: Zenith Bank advanced £500,000 to Rapid Construction Ltd for the specific purpose of purchasing materials for a building project. Rapid Construction diverted the money to pay existing debts instead. The bank has now discovered that Rapid Construction is insolvent.
questions.stem: Which of the following best explains the bank's position under Quistclose trust principles?

options.A: The bank has only a personal contractual claim ranking equally with other unsecured creditors
options.B: The bank holds the money on trust for the purposes specified, with a resulting trust back to the bank if the purpose fails, giving proprietary protection
options.C: The bank automatically becomes a secured creditor with priority over all other debts
options.D: The bank's rights depend on whether Rapid Construction specifically promised to use the money for the stated purpose
options.E: The bank loses all rights once the money is mixed with other company funds

questions.explanation: B is correct. Quistclose trust principles (Barclays Bank v Quistclose, confirmed in Twinsectra v Yardley) establish that money lent for a specific purpose is held on trust; if the purpose fails, a resulting trust arises in favour of the lender, protecting the bank's position as a beneficiary in insolvency rather than as an unsecured creditor. A is wrong because the bank's position is proprietary, not merely contractual. C is incorrect; the bank is not automatically a secured creditor but rather a beneficiary of the resulting trust. D is wrong because mutual intention regarding the purpose is tested objectively from the parties' conduct, not solely by promise. E is incorrect because the Quistclose trust can subsist despite mixing if the purpose fails.

questions.scenario: Sarah lent £50,000 to her brother Marcus under a written agreement stating 'Marcus will repay this sum on demand with 3% interest'. Sarah later becomes insolvent. Marcus claims the £50,000 should be treated as trust property, giving Sarah a proprietary claim that would rank ahead of her other creditors.
questions.stem: Is Marcus's argument that the relationship creates a trust likely to succeed?

options.A: Yes, because any loan agreement creates an equitable obligation that constitutes a trust
options.B: No, because a loan with a fixed interest rate creates a debt relationship, not a trust, giving Sarah only a personal contractual claim that ranks pari passu with other unsecured debts
options.C: Yes, because Sarah and Marcus are family members and family loans are always trusts
options.D: No, because written agreements cannot create trusts unless they use the word 'trust'
options.E: Yes, because Marcus's obligation to repay is secured, which converts the loan into a trust

questions.explanation: B is correct. A loan agreement creates a contractual debt relationship giving the creditor only a personal claim, not proprietary rights. In insolvency, Sarah ranks as an unsecured creditor pari passu with others, not as a beneficiary with proprietary protection. The distinction between trust (proprietary rights) and debt (personal rights) is fundamental. A is wrong because not all loans create trusts; a conventional loan creates only debt. C is incorrect; family relationships do not automatically create trusts absent clear intention. D is false; trusts need not use the word 'trust' to be valid (Paul v Constance), but this loan shows no intention to create a trust. E is wrong because the existence or absence of security does not convert a debt into a trust.

questions.scenario: Elite Customs Brokerage Ltd received advance payments totalling £80,000 from import/export clients for processing customs documentation. The company's finance director immediately opened a separate bank account and deposited all advance payments into it, with instructions that the money should only be used for the clients' customs services. The company is now in financial difficulty, and clients claim the money in the separate account is held on trust.
questions.stem: Are the clients likely to succeed in arguing that a trust arose over the advance payments?

options.A: No, because advance payments to a business are always treated as general company debts, not trusts
options.B: Yes, because the company segregated the money into a separate account designated for a specific purpose, which creates a trust (Re Kayford principle)
options.C: No, because only written contracts create trusts over money
options.D: Yes, but only if the clients can prove the finance director personally intended to create a trust
options.E: No, because the company's insolvency means the trust cannot have existed when the payments were made

questions.explanation: B is correct. Re Kayford establishes that where a company segregates advance customer payments into a separate account designated for specific purposes, a trust arises from the act of segregation itself, regardless of formal trust language. This protects clients as beneficiaries in insolvency. The certainty of intention is satisfied objectively by the segregation conduct. A is wrong because advance payments can create trusts, not just debts. C is incorrect; trusts need not be in writing unless they concern land (s53(1)(b)) or disposition of equitable interests (s53(1)(c)). D is false because certainty of intention is tested objectively, not by subjective intention of one party. E is wrong because the trust arose when the account was segregated, not affected by later insolvency.

questions.scenario: James holds 5,000 ordinary shares in TechCore Ltd on trust for his niece Emma. Emma orally directs James: 'Transfer the shares to my husband David'. James verbally agrees and instructs the share registrar to transfer the shares to David's name without any written instruction from Emma.
questions.stem: Is the transfer of Emma's equitable interest to David valid?

options.A: Yes, because James as trustee can transfer equitable interests orally on the beneficiary's instructions
options.B: No, because under s53(1)(c) LPA 1925, any disposition of a subsisting equitable interest must be in writing signed by the person disposing (here Emma) or their agent
options.C: Yes, because this is a transfer of legal title, not an equitable interest, so s53(1)(c) does not apply
options.D: Yes, because Emma's oral direction to the trustee is sufficient evidence of her intention to dispose
options.E: No, because equitable interests in shares cannot be disposed of at all without the trustee's written consent

questions.explanation: B is correct. Grey v IRC and Vandervell v IRC establish that s53(1)(c) requires any disposition of a subsisting equitable interest to be evidenced in writing signed by the person disposing or their agent. Emma's oral direction is insufficient; she must sign a written disposition. A is wrong because trustees cannot validly act on oral directions to dispose of equitable interests. C is incorrect; the registrar's transfer of legal title is the effect of the disposition, but the equitable interest is what Emma is disposing of, triggering s53(1)(c). D is false because oral intention, however clear, does not satisfy the statutory writing requirement. E is wrong; equitable interests can be disposed of provided formality requirements are met.

questions.scenario: In 2023, David promised his daughter Lucy that he would give her his valuable antique watch, but he never transferred it. In his will made in 2024, David appointed Lucy as his executor. David died in 2025, and the legal title to the watch now vests in Lucy as executor of the estate.
questions.stem: Can Lucy keep the watch as her own property under Strong v Bird principles?

options.A: No, because a promise to give is never enough to transfer property; Lucy must return the watch to the estate
options.B: Yes, because Strong v Bird provides that an imperfect gift can be perfected when the donee is appointed executor and legal title vests in them on the testator's death, provided there was a prior firm intention to give
options.C: No, because the watch was not actually transferred during David's lifetime, so the doctrine of constitution is breached
options.D: Yes, only if Lucy can prove that David wrote down the promise to give her the watch
options.E: No, because an executor must hold all estate property on trust for beneficiaries and cannot benefit personally from their office

questions.explanation: B is correct. Strong v Bird establishes that an imperfect gift (an unfulfilled promise without delivery) can be perfected if the donor appoints the donee as executor; when legal title vests in the executor on the donor's death, the donee obtains the property beneficially, provided there was a continuous firm intention to give. David's promise in 2023 and Lucy's appointment as executor in the 2024 will satisfy the doctrine. A is wrong because Strong v Bird is an exception to the rule that imperfect gifts fail. C is incorrect; constitution can occur via Strong v Bird despite earlier non-transfer. D is false; writing is not required for Strong v Bird to operate (though it may assist proof). E is wrong; an executor can benefit from a gift perfected by the doctrine.

questions.scenario: Patrick, who had suffered a heart attack, told his friend Mira: 'I think I'm dying. I want you to have my collection of rare books. Take these keys — they open the cabinet in my study where the first edition collection is kept, and also the safe deposit box at the bank where I keep the deeds to my house.' Patrick recovered fully and lived for another five years, during which he made clear he wanted the books and house back.
questions.stem: What is Mira's legal position regarding the books and house?

options.A: Mira acquired absolute ownership of both the books and the house because a valid donatio mortis causa occurred
options.B: Mira's gifts of the books and house are both valid donatio mortis causa because she received delivery of dominion (the keys) and Patrick believed he was dying, although her ownership is conditional on Patrick's death and can be recalled if he recovers
options.C: Mira acquired only the books because actual physical delivery was given, but the house cannot be the subject of a donatio mortis causa without a written conveyance
options.D: Mira acquired nothing because Patrick recovered, which voided any conditional gift made in contemplation of death
options.E: Mira must hold both the books and house on trust for Patrick's heirs because a donatio mortis causa cannot transfer absolute ownership

questions.explanation: B is correct. Donatio mortis causa requires (1) gift made in contemplation of death, (2) delivery of dominion, and (3) conditional on death occurring. Patrick's belief he was dying and transfer of keys delivering dominion over both the books (in the cabinet) and the house (via access to deeds and safe deposit box, applying Sen v Headley) satisfies the doctrine. However, the gift is revocable and fails if the donor survives, as here. Mira had a conditional right that was defeated by Patrick's recovery. A is wrong because donatio mortis causa is conditional on death. C is incorrect; keys giving dominion over documents establishing ownership can constitute delivery even for land (Sen v Headley). D is partially correct in result but wrong in reasoning; the gift fails because it was conditional, not because recovery automatically voids all prior transactions. E is false; a valid donatio mortis causa transfers beneficial ownership, though conditionally.

questions.scenario: Victoria intended to settle her farmland on trust for her two daughters. On 15 March, she signed a transfer form (TR1) and sent it to her solicitor with instructions to register it at the Land Registry. The solicitor received the form on 18 March but did not submit it to the Land Registry until 22 March. Victoria died on 20 March. Her executor now questions whether the settlement was properly constituted.
questions.stem: Was the trust in the farmland properly constituted by Victoria's death?

options.A: No, because the transfer was not registered at the Land Registry before Victoria's death
options.B: Yes, because under Mascall v Mascall, applying Re Rose principles, once the transferor has done everything in their power (executing and sending the transfer form), the transfer is constituted in equity, even if registration occurs later or after death
options.C: No, because land cannot be transferred in trust without written registration being completed during the donor's lifetime
options.D: Yes, but only because Victoria's executor completed the registration after her death, which validated the earlier transfer
options.E: No, because the solicitor's delay in submitting the form means Victoria did not do everything in her power

questions.explanation: B is correct. Re Rose and Mascall v Mascall establish that where a settlor has done everything in their power to transfer property, equity regards the transfer as complete, even if registration is not yet finished. Victoria executed and delivered the transfer form to her solicitor, doing all she could; registration by the Land Registry is the other party's act. The trust was constituted before her death. A is wrong because registration is not required for constitution in equity once everything in the settlor's power is done. C is incorrect; trusts of land need not be registered to be valid in equity (though registration provides legal title). D is false; the trust was already constituted on 18 March when the solicitor received the form. E is wrong; the solicitor's actions are outside Victoria's control, so her obligation was only to initiate transfer properly.

questions.tag: Milroy v Lord and imperfect gifts
questions.scenario: Anthony intended to give his vintage car to his son Trevor as a gift. He handed Trevor a handwritten note saying 'This car is now yours' and told him 'Consider it a gift; I've given you a note as evidence.' However, Anthony retained the car's registration document in his name and took no steps to transfer legal title. The next week, Anthony decided he wanted the car back.
questions.stem: Can Anthony recover the car, or has Trevor acquired ownership?

options.A: Trevor owns the car because Anthony's written note constitutes a sufficient declaration of trust for Trevor's benefit
options.B: Anthony can recover the car because Milroy v Lord establishes that an imperfect gift fails—the three modes of creating a trust are transfer to trustee, self-declaration of trust, or covenant, and Anthony achieved none of these; equity will not perfect an imperfect gift
options.C: Trevor owns the car because Anthony's oral gift combined with the written note is sufficient delivery of dominion
options.D: Anthony can recover the car only if he can prove Trevor agreed the note was non-binding
options.E: Trevor owns the car because a gift of a car does not require legal formalities and Anthony's intention is sufficient

questions.explanation: B is correct. Milroy v Lord establishes that gifts can be created only via three modes: (1) transfer of legal title to a trustee, (2) self-declaration of trust, or (3) covenant to transfer. Anthony did neither—he retained legal title, made no declaration of trust, and gave no covenant. His written note is evidence of intention but not a legally effective transfer. Equity will not perfect the imperfect gift. A is wrong because a note saying 'this is yours' is not a declaration of trust (which would require him to hold on trust for Trevor). C is incorrect; a note alone is not delivery of dominion without transfer of legal title. D is false because the legal effect does not depend on Trevor's agreement; the transfer was incomplete. E is wrong; chattels require legal transfer or another Milroy mode; intention alone is insufficient.

questions.scenario: Professor Eleanor Blackwell established a trust in her will with £500,000 'for the advancement of intellectual debate on contemporary philosophy' with no named beneficiaries. The trustee is uncertain how to distribute the income and seeks court guidance. No charitable status was sought.
questions.stem: What is the most likely judicial outcome?

options.A: The trust will be valid and the trustee should spend income on philosophy seminars and publications
options.B: The trust is void under the beneficiary principle because it is a non-charitable purpose trust with no ascertainable beneficiaries to enforce it (Morice v Bishop of Durham, Re Astor)
options.C: The trust is valid because purposes, even without named individuals, are always enforceable under Re Denley
options.D: The trust is void because it has no charitable status, but the money will be held on resulting trust for the donor's estate
options.E: The trust is valid if the trustee can identify at least one individual who benefits from intellectual debate

questions.explanation: B is correct. Re Astor and the beneficiary principle establish that non-charitable purpose trusts are void because there are no ascertainable beneficiaries with standing to enforce the trust. 'Advancement of intellectual debate' is an abstract purpose; no individual is directly identified as a beneficiary. The principle from Morice v Bishop of Durham requires enforceable trusts to have beneficiaries. A is wrong because the trust is void, not valid. C is incorrect; Re Denley applies only where a purpose trust benefits ascertainable individuals directly (e.g., persons using a sports ground), not abstract philosophical advancement. D is partially correct (resulting trust outcome) but wrong about charitable status being the sole reason; the reason is the absence of ascertainable beneficiaries. E is wrong because identifying incidental beneficiaries of a purpose does not make the purpose trust valid under the beneficiary principle.

questions.scenario: TechWorkers Ltd established a trust with £300,000 'for the maintenance and upkeep of the staff recreation ground and sports facilities for the benefit and enjoyment of the company's employees, present and future.' The trust deed named no specific individuals but stated that the trustee should maintain the ground and facilities for all employees. Several employees have now sued the trustee for failing to maintain the facilities adequately.
questions.stem: Will the employees succeed in arguing that they can enforce the trust?

options.A: No, because a purpose trust with no named beneficiaries cannot be enforced by anyone under the beneficiary principle
options.B: No, because purpose trusts cannot benefit future employees, only current employees at the time the trust was created
options.C: Yes, under Re Denley principles—although expressed as a purpose ('maintenance of facilities'), the trust directly benefits ascertainable individuals (the company's employees) who can enforce it
options.D: Yes, but only if the employees can prove the trustee intended them to be enforcers when the trust was created
options.E: No, because purpose trusts are only enforceable if they are charitable, and a staff recreation ground is not a charitable purpose

questions.explanation: C is correct. Re Denley establishes an exception to the beneficiary principle: where a trust is expressed as a purpose but the purpose is for the benefit of ascertainable individuals (employees), those individuals can enforce it as a trust for persons. The employees here are identifiable members of the company, directly benefited by the facilities. A is wrong because Re Denley provides an exception for purpose trusts benefiting ascertainable persons. B is incorrect; Re Denley does not require all beneficiaries to be named at inception, only ascertainable. D is false because enforceability depends on the objective structure (purpose for ascertainable class), not the trustee's subjective intent. E is wrong; the trust need not be charitable; Re Denley applies to non-charitable purpose trusts benefiting identified individuals.

questions.scenario: A trust was established with £1,000,000 'for the advancement of education through funding scholarships for students from low-income families to attend university.' The settlor wishes to know if the trust qualifies as a charity under the Charities Act 2011.
questions.stem: Does this trust likely qualify as a charitable trust?

options.A: No, because scholarships are benefits to individuals, not to the public, and therefore fail the public benefit test
options.B: Yes, because it falls within the head of 'advancement of education' under s3(1) Charities Act 2011, and the public benefit requirement is presumed for educational trusts
options.C: Yes, because it falls within 'advancement of education' and appears to satisfy the public benefit requirement by benefiting a significant class (low-income students) who would not otherwise have educational access, though this must be demonstrated rather than presumed
options.D: No, because restricting scholarships to low-income students is unlawful discrimination and makes the trust non-charitable
options.E: Yes, if at least one student from outside the settlor's family receives a scholarship, making the benefit public

questions.explanation: C is correct. The trust falls within the 'advancement of education' head under s3(1) Charities Act 2011. However, s4 provides that there is NO presumption of public benefit; it must be demonstrated. Low-income students would not have university access without such scholarships, so the public benefit (addressing a significant educational gap for a substantial class) can be shown. B is incorrect because there is no presumption of public benefit. A is wrong because trusts benefiting individuals can satisfy public benefit if they address a significant social need. D is false; restricting scholarships to low-income students is not unlawful discrimination but rather a legitimate charitable focus. E is wrong; public benefit is not established merely by including one non-family member.

questions.scenario: The Riverside Social Club, an unincorporated members' association, received a £100,000 gift from a patron 'to benefit the club and its members.' The club has 200 current members bound by a written constitution setting out their rights and obligations. The association is later dissolved, and the members dispute who owns the fund.
questions.stem: Under Re Recher principles, how should the £100,000 be treated?

options.A: The gift vested in the club as a separate legal entity and is held in its absolute name
options.B: The gift belongs to the club's trustees personally, as unincorporated associations cannot own property
options.C: Under Re Recher principles (contract-holding theory), the £100,000 is held as an accretion to the members' funds, with each current member having a contractual right to share in the fund according to the constitution; future members admitted after the gift have no claim
options.D: The gift is held on trust for all current and future members of the club in perpetuity
options.E: The gift automatically becomes charitable because it benefits an unincorporated association

questions.explanation: C is correct. Re Recher established that where property is gifted to an unincorporated association, it is treated as an accretion to members' funds held on the 'contract-holding' theory: each member's rights derive from the association's constitution (a contract between members). The gift belongs to current members as defined by the constitution; future members are not bound by this prior contract and have no claim. A is wrong because unincorporated associations are not separate legal entities. B is incorrect; the property is not held in the trustees' personal names but belongs to the members collectively. D is false; the gift does not extend to future members beyond the original accretion rule. E is wrong; gifts to associations are not automatically charitable merely because they benefit a group.

questions.scenario: Sarah was appointed trustee of her uncle's estate. A valuable commercial lease held in the trust came up for renewal. The landlord refused to renew the lease for the trust beneficiary. Sarah, using her position as trustee and knowledge of the lease terms, approached the landlord and had the lease renewed in her own name instead.
questions.stem: What principle is breached and what is the consequence for Sarah?

options.A: Sarah has breached the no-conflict rule; the lease will be held on constructive trust for the beneficiary
options.B: Sarah has breached the no-profit rule; she must account for all profits made from the lease
options.C: Sarah has committed self-dealing; the transaction is voidable at the instance of the beneficiary
options.D: Sarah has failed in her duty of care; she must compensate the trust for lost opportunity
options.E: Sarah has breached fiduciary duty; she may retain the lease if she can prove it was commercially fair

questions.explanation: A is correct. This scenario directly follows Keech v Sandford (1726): a trustee cannot take for themselves an opportunity arising from their position as trustee. The strict rule is that a fiduciary must not place personal interest in conflict with fiduciary duty (no-conflict rule from Bray v Ford). The lease is held on constructive trust for the beneficiary. B is incorrect because the no-profit rule applies to unauthorised benefits from the position (like the Boardman v Phipps scenario of using fiduciary information), not to the taking of opportunities themselves. C is incorrect because self-dealing specifically concerns a trustee purchasing trust property; here Sarah took the lease opportunity, not purchased from the trust. D is incorrect because this is not merely a breach of the duty of care (s1 TA 2000) but a fundamental breach of the no-conflict rule. E is incorrect because Keech v Sandford establishes that fairness is irrelevant; the rule is strict and applies regardless of whether the transaction was fair.

questions.scenario: Michael is a trustee of a trust that holds a large portfolio of commercial properties. While reviewing the trust's property records, Michael learns that a neighbouring plot of land is about to be sold at a significant discount due to a forced sale. Using this information and industry contacts he has developed in his capacity as trustee property manager, Michael purchases the plot personally at the discounted price and then resells it three months later at market value for a substantial personal profit.
questions.stem: Under the no-profit rule, what is the outcome for Michael?

options.A: Michael is liable to account for the entire profit to the trust because he used information obtained as trustee to make an unauthorised profit
options.B: Michael can retain the profit because the information came from public sources, not confidential trust documents
options.C: Michael must repay only the discount amount he obtained, not the entire resale profit
options.D: Michael is not liable because he acted in good faith and was not intending to benefit at the trust's expense
options.E: Michael has committed self-dealing and the transaction is voidable at the instance of the beneficiary

questions.explanation: A is correct. Boardman v Phipps [1967] establishes the no-profit rule: a fiduciary must not obtain unauthorised benefit from their position. Michael used information acquired through his role as trustee (knowledge of the property market and contacts developed in that capacity) to make a personal profit. He must account for the entire profit to the trust. The fiduciary's good faith is irrelevant; the rule is strict. B is incorrect because the information came through Michael's position as trustee property manager, even though the market information itself may be publicly available—the opportunity arose from the fiduciary relationship. C is incorrect because liability extends to the full unauthorised profit, not just the discount obtained. D is incorrect because good faith and intention are irrelevant under the strict no-profit rule established in Boardman v Phipps. E is incorrect because self-dealing specifically concerns purchasing trust property; this case involves taking an opportunity that arose from the fiduciary position, which is governed by the no-profit rule rather than the self-dealing rule.

questions.scenario: Emma is trustee of a trust that holds a valuable rare book collection. The trust is in financial difficulty and decides to sell some of the collection. Emma, knowing the collection well, identifies a first edition as being undervalued at £5,000. She purchases the book from the trust at that price and later sells it privately for £15,000.
questions.stem: What is the legal consequence of Emma's purchase?

options.A: The transaction is valid because Emma paid a fair market price for the book
options.B: The transaction is voidable at the instance of the beneficiary regardless of whether the price was fair
options.C: Emma must account for the profit of £10,000 as an unauthorised benefit
options.D: The transaction is valid if Emma can prove she did not know the true value at the time of purchase
options.E: Emma has committed accessory liability and must compensate the trust for losses

questions.explanation: B is correct. The self-dealing rule (from Tito v Waddell (No 2)) provides that a trustee cannot purchase trust property, and the transaction is voidable at the instance of the beneficiary regardless of the fairness of the price. The rule is strict and does not depend on whether the trustee acted in good faith or obtained a bargain. A is incorrect because fairness of price is irrelevant under the self-dealing rule; any purchase of trust property by the trustee is voidable. C is incorrect because while Emma may also owe profits under the no-profit rule, the primary consequence for self-dealing is that the transaction is voidable, not merely that she must account for profits. D is incorrect because the trustee's subjective knowledge of value is irrelevant; the self-dealing rule is strict and operates regardless of the trustee's state of mind. E is incorrect because accessory liability concerns third parties who assist in breaches of trust, not a trustee's own breach through self-dealing.

questions.scenario: David is a trustee who wishes to misapply £50,000 from the trust fund for his personal use. He approaches Jennifer, a solicitor with knowledge of trust law, and asks her to facilitate the transfer. Jennifer drafts the necessary transfer documents and arranges for the funds to be paid to David's personal account. Jennifer was aware that David was not authorised to make such a withdrawal and consciously proceeded regardless. The transfer is completed and David disappears.
questions.stem: Is Jennifer liable for accessory liability (dishonest assistance) in this breach of trust?

options.A: Yes, because she provided assistance to the breach of trust and her conduct was dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people
options.B: No, because Jennifer is a solicitor and professionals are exempt from accessory liability
options.C: Yes, but only if David acted with dishonest intent; Jennifer's own state of mind is irrelevant
options.D: No, because accessory liability only applies to trustees, not third parties
options.E: Yes, but Jennifer is protected because she acted on the instructions of a trustee

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995], accessory liability requires: (1) a trust or fiduciary relationship, (2) a breach of that relationship, (3) assistance by a third party, and (4) dishonesty. Here all four elements are present. Jennifer assisted the breach of trust by David and the conduct was dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people (she consciously proceeded despite knowing the withdrawal was unauthorised). The test for dishonesty is objective (Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017]): would ordinary decent people regard Jennifer's conduct as dishonest? Yes—knowingly facilitating an unauthorised misapplication of trust funds. B is incorrect because professional status does not exempt someone from accessory liability; if anything, a solicitor's knowledge makes the dishonesty clearer. C is incorrect because dishonesty is assessed objectively and the third party's (Jennifer's) dishonesty is essential to liability under Royal Brunei v Tan. D is incorrect because accessory liability is a stranger liability doctrine and specifically applies to third parties who are not themselves the trustee. E is incorrect because following instructions from a trustee does not protect a third party who knows the instructions are to breach the trust.

questions.scenario: Robert is trustee of a pension fund with £2 million in assets. The trust documents give him power to invest in any assets he deems appropriate. Robert, who has no formal financial training and limited investment experience, decides to make a large investment in highly speculative technology shares without consulting any financial adviser, conducting research, or considering the trust's beneficiaries' needs. The investment subsequently fails, causing significant loss to the trust.
questions.stem: Has Robert breached his duty of care under the Trustee Act 2000?

options.A: No, because the trust documents gave him power to invest in any assets he chose
options.B: Yes, because the duty of care requires trustees to act with reasonable care considering their knowledge and experience, and Robert failed to take proper advice before investing
options.C: No, because the investment decision itself was not imprudent—speculative investments can be appropriate
options.D: Yes, but only because Robert is not a professional trustee and the standard is strict liability regardless
options.E: No, because s3 TA 2000 gives trustees an absolute power of investment without any duty of care qualification

questions.explanation: B is correct. Section 1 of the Trustee Act 2000 imposes a statutory duty of care: a trustee must act with such care and skill as is reasonable in the circumstances, having regard to special knowledge or experience the trustee possesses. Robert, an inexperienced trustee, made a speculative investment without taking proper advice (required by s5 TA 2000 before investing) and without considering the standard investment criteria (s4 TA 2000: suitability and diversification). This is a breach of the duty of care. A is incorrect because the power of investment under s3 TA 2000 is subject to the duty of care under s1 and the standard investment criteria under s4; the power is not absolute. C is incorrect because the duty of care is breached by the failure to take proper advice and consider the beneficiaries' needs, not merely by the nature of the investment itself. D is incorrect because the standard of care is not strict liability; it is reasonable care appropriate to the trustee's knowledge and experience. While a professional trustee faces a higher standard, Robert's breach is based on his failure to take proper advice, not on strict liability. E is incorrect because s3 TA 2000 is expressly made subject to s4 (standard investment criteria) and both s1 and s5 (duty of care and duty to take advice).

questions.scenario: Claire is trustee of a trust with £1 million. She wishes to invest £400,000 in speculative micro-cap biotechnology shares that offer potential for high returns but carry substantial risk. The beneficiary is a 75-year-old retired person with limited income. Claire has not sought any investment advice and has not considered diversification of the portfolio. The trust documents contain no specific investment instructions.
questions.stem: Under the standard investment criteria in s4 TA 2000, what is the issue with Claire's proposed investment?

options.A: The investment is unsuitable because it does not match the suitability criterion given the beneficiary's circumstances and the requirement for diversification is breached
options.B: The investment is suitable because the trust documents give no restrictions and s3 TA 2000 allows any investment
options.C: The investment is unsuitable only if Claire fails to take proper advice; the unsuitability of speculative shares is irrelevant
options.D: The investment is suitable because high-return investments are always appropriate for long-term wealth building
options.E: The investment is unsuitable solely because the beneficiary is elderly; age is the determining factor for suitability

questions.explanation: A is correct. Section 4 TA 2000 sets the standard investment criteria: the trustee must have regard to (1) the suitability of the investment for the trust and (2) the need for diversification. Here, the proposed investment fails both criteria. The suitability criterion requires consideration of the trust's purposes and the beneficiary's circumstances; a £400,000 concentration in high-risk micro-cap shares is unsuitable for a retired 75-year-old with limited income who needs stable returns. The diversification criterion is breached by placing 40% of the portfolio in one speculative sector. B is incorrect because s3 TA 2000's general power of investment is expressly made subject to s4's standard investment criteria; the power is not absolute. C is incorrect because while s5 requires proper advice, the unsuitability of the investment is a breach of s4 independently; the failure to take advice is a separate breach. D is incorrect because high-return potential does not automatically make an investment suitable; suitability requires consideration of the beneficiary's circumstances and diversification requirements. E is incorrect because while age is relevant to suitability, it is not the sole determining factor; the overall mismatch between the investment profile and the beneficiary's needs is the issue.

questions.scenario: Jasper's accountant fraudulently steals £20,000 from Jasper's bank account. The accountant immediately uses the stolen money to purchase a specific identifiable plot of land registered in the accountant's name. Jasper discovers the theft six months later and seeks to recover the land.
questions.stem: Can Jasper trace the stolen money at common law and recover the land?

options.A: No, because common law tracing requires a fiduciary relationship, which Jasper does not have with the accountant
options.B: Yes, because the money was used in a clean substitution to purchase an identifiable asset without mixing with other funds
options.C: No, because the accountant's ownership of the land means the theft is complete and tracing is impossible
options.D: Yes, because Jasper has been deprived of money and can claim any asset the accountant purchased
options.E: No, because common law tracing can only follow cash and cannot follow property purchases

questions.explanation: B is correct. Common law tracing, as established in cases like Banque Belge v Hambrouck [1921] and Lipkin Gorman v Karpnale [1991], allows tracing of property through clean substitutions (one form of property directly exchanged for another without mixing). Here the stolen money was used directly to purchase a specific identifiable plot of land without being mixed with other funds. This is a clean substitution and common law tracing applies; Jasper can claim the land. A is incorrect because common law tracing does not require a fiduciary relationship; it operates between any original owner and their property. C is incorrect because the accountant's legal ownership does not prevent tracing remedies; the question of legal ownership is distinct from the ability to trace. D is incorrect because the ability to claim is not simply based on Jasper's loss; it requires successful tracing to an identifiable asset that has been purchased. E is incorrect because common law tracing does follow money into real property (land) where the purchase is made in a clean substitution and the property remains identifiable.

questions.scenario: Hassan is trustee of a trust with £30,000 in a separate bank account. Hassan wrongfully mixes this trust money with his personal savings, depositing both into a single account containing £20,000 of Hassan's own money, bringing the total to £50,000. Six months later, Hassan withdraws £15,000 for personal purposes and the account balance is reduced to £35,000. The beneficiary seeks to trace the trust money.
questions.stem: Can the beneficiary trace through the mixed fund and what principle applies?

options.A: No, because common law tracing is defeated by mixing and the money is now unidentifiable
options.B: Yes, because there is a fiduciary relationship between trustee and beneficiary, allowing equitable tracing through mixed funds
options.C: Yes, but only for the £15,000 withdrawn; the remainder cannot be traced once mixed
options.D: No, because Hassan has a prior claim to his own funds before any beneficiary claim
options.E: Yes, but the beneficiary can only recover Hassan's own money and not the trust money once mixed

questions.explanation: B is correct. Equitable tracing, established in Re Hallett's Estate (1880), allows tracing through mixed funds when there is a fiduciary relationship. Hassan is trustee (fiduciary) and the beneficiary has a beneficial interest (fiduciary relationship). Equitable tracing applies and allows the beneficiary to claim a charge over the mixed fund or make elections regarding what was spent. The presence of the fiduciary relationship is the key difference from common law tracing, which cannot follow through mixing. A is incorrect because equitable tracing (not common law tracing) is available precisely because of the fiduciary relationship; mixing does not defeat equitable tracing. C is incorrect because equitable tracing allows recovery through the entire mixed fund, not just from withdrawals; the principle is that the beneficiary can claim a proportionate share of the mixed account. D is incorrect because equitable principles protect the beneficiary's interest ahead of the trustee's personal interest; the trustee cannot claim priority for their own funds when they have breached fiduciary duty by mixing. E is incorrect because equitable tracing allows the beneficiary to claim trust property or its proceeds despite mixing; the whole point is that mixing does not defeat equitable claims.

questions.scenario: Patricia is trustee of a trust with £40,000. She mixes this trust money with £10,000 of her own personal funds in a single account. Patricia then uses £25,000 from the mixed account to purchase shares which subsequently increase in value to £60,000. Patricia then spends £20,000 from the remaining mixed account balance on personal luxury goods (now worthless). The beneficiary seeks to trace.
questions.stem: Under Re Oatway, which asset does the beneficiary have priority to claim?

options.A: The beneficiary can claim only the remaining cash in the mixed account proportionate to their contribution
options.B: The beneficiary can claim the shares because Patricia is presumed to have spent her own money first on the luxury goods
options.C: The beneficiary cannot claim the shares because Patricia's personal money was spent first
options.D: The beneficiary must share the shares proportionately with Patricia because both contributed to the mixed fund
options.E: The beneficiary can claim either the shares or the remaining cash, but Patricia gets first choice

questions.explanation: B is correct. Re Oatway [1903] establishes a beneficiary-protecting presumption: where a trustee mixes funds and makes a purchase from the mixed account, if the trustee's own money is subsequently dissipated or spent, the beneficiary can claim the purchased asset. The presumption is that the trustee spent their own money first (only when beneficial to the beneficiary). Here Patricia spent £20,000 on worthless luxury goods after purchasing shares. The presumption is that this £20,000 came from Patricia's own funds, therefore the beneficiary can claim the shares which have increased in value. This is beneficial to the beneficiary and the presumption applies. A is incorrect because the beneficiary is not limited to a proportionate claim; under Re Oatway, they can claim the profitable asset when the presumption applies. C is incorrect because the presumption operates in the opposite way; it presumes Patricia spent her own money, allowing the beneficiary to claim the purchased asset. D is incorrect because the beneficiary does not share proportionately under Re Oatway; the presumption favors the beneficiary by allowing them to claim the purchased asset. E is incorrect because neither party has a choice; the presumption in Re Oatway operates objectively to attribute spending to the trustee's own money when beneficial to the beneficiary.

questions.scenario: A Ponzi scheme operator opens a bank account and receives deposits from ten innocent investors, each depositing £5,000 at different times over six months. The operator then withdraws £25,000 from the account to pay earlier investors, maintaining the illusion of returns. When the scheme collapses, the account contains only £25,000. The investors sue to recover their money from the remaining balance.
questions.stem: How should the £25,000 be distributed among the innocent investors?

options.A: Clayton's Case (first in, first out) applies and the first investors to deposit money have first claim to the remaining £25,000
options.B: Clayton's Case applies and the last depositors to contribute have first claim because their money was deposited most recently
options.C: Clayton's Case applies and all investors must share pari passu proportionate to their contributions
options.D: Clayton's Case does not apply because there is no fiduciary relationship between the investors and the operator
options.E: Clayton's Case applies but is displaced by Barlow Clowes v Vaughan in favour of pari passu distribution among innocent parties

questions.explanation: E is correct. Clayton's Case (1816) normally applies the first-in-first-out rule to trace money through mixed accounts. However, Barlow Clowes v Vaughan [1992] established that Clayton's Case can be displaced when it would be impractical or unjust, particularly where multiple innocent parties have contributed to a mixed fund (as in a Ponzi scheme with many victims). In such scenarios, pari passu (proportionate sharing) applies instead. Here, applying Clayton's Case would unfairly advantage early depositors; pari passu allows each of the ten investors to recover their £5,000 share of the remaining £25,000 pro-rata. A is incorrect because while Clayton's Case normally applies to mixed accounts, it is displaced in this multi-party innocent contributor scenario. B is incorrect because Clayton's Case uses first-in-first-out, not last-in-first-out. C is incorrect because Clayton's Case itself does not mandate pari passu; pari passu is the alternative where Clayton's Case is displaced. D is incorrect because the absence of a fiduciary relationship does not prevent equitable tracing and Clayton's Case from applying between innocent parties to a mixed fund.

questions.scenario: Olivia is a trustee who misappropriates £100,000 from the trust and uses it to purchase a vintage car from Derek, a second-hand car dealer. Derek knows nothing of the trust, receives the money in good faith, and has no reason to suspect it is trust property. Derek honestly believes he is selling to Olivia personally. Weeks later, the beneficiary discovers the misappropriation and seeks to recover the car from Derek.
questions.stem: Can the beneficiary trace and recover the car from Derek?

options.A: Yes, because the beneficiary can trace trust property regardless of whether the recipient is a bona fide purchaser
options.B: No, because Derek is a bona fide purchaser for value without notice and is therefore protected by the equitable defence
options.C: Yes, but Derek can retain the car if he pays back the £100,000 to the beneficiary
options.D: No, because Derek is a dealer and is presumed to have greater knowledge of trust law
options.E: Yes, because the beneficiary has equitable ownership of the car even if Derek is in possession

questions.explanation: B is correct. The bona fide purchaser for value without notice (often called 'equity's darling') is a complete defence to equitable tracing claims. Derek is a bona fide purchaser: he paid value (received the money from Olivia), acted in good faith (had no knowledge the money was trust property), and had no notice of Olivia's breach of trust. The beneficiary cannot trace against Derek or recover the car. This is a fundamental principle that innocent third-party purchasers are protected from equitable claims. A is incorrect because while beneficiaries can normally trace trust property, the bona fide purchaser defence is a complete exception to this rule. C is incorrect because Derek is not required to refund the money; the bona fide purchaser defence is absolute, not conditional on payment. D is incorrect because Derek's profession (car dealer) is irrelevant; the defence applies to any innocent purchaser for value without notice, regardless of their knowledge or profession. E is incorrect because equitable ownership does not prevail against a bona fide purchaser for value without notice; this defence is precisely designed to protect such purchasers from equitable claims.

questions.scenario: A financial investment company collects money from 30 investors into a mixed client account. The company's records show each investor's supposed contribution and entitlement, but the account has become thoroughly mixed. The company misappropriates and disappears. The remaining balance in the account is £120,000, but the total claimed by investors is £180,000. No single investor's funds can be separately identified.
questions.stem: How should the £120,000 be distributed to the 30 claimants?

options.A: The first investors to deposit funds receive full claims first; the later investors receive proportionate shares of the remainder
options.B: Each investor receives an equal share of £4,000 regardless of their claimed contribution
options.C: Each investor recovers a proportionate share of the £120,000 based on their claimed contribution ratio to the £180,000 total
options.D: Only the investors who can prove earliest deposit dates are entitled to recover any funds
options.E: The distribution follows Clayton's Case (first in, first out) strict ordering

questions.explanation: C is correct. Barlow Clowes v Vaughan [1992] established that when multiple innocent parties contribute to a mixed fund and Clayton's Case would be impractical or unjust, pari passu (proportionate sharing) applies instead. Here, 30 innocent investors each contributed to a thoroughly mixed account. Clayton's Case cannot be fairly applied (records are mixed and time sequence may be unclear). Instead, pari passu distribution applies: each investor's claim is treated as a proportion of the total claimed (£120,000 / £180,000 = 66.7% recovery). An investor claiming £6,000 would recover £4,000 (66.7% of £6,000). This is fairer to innocent parties than first-in-first-out. A is incorrect because pari passu specifically displaces the first-in-first-out rule of Clayton's Case. B is incorrect because equal distribution ignores each investor's actual contribution; pari passu is proportionate, not equal. D is incorrect because proof of deposit date is not required for pari passu distribution; all innocent contributors share proportionately. E is incorrect because Barlow Clowes v Vaughan explicitly displaces Clayton's Case in favour of pari passu when multiple innocent parties are involved.

questions.scenario: In 1961, Mr Vandervell transferred shares in his family company to the Royal College of Surgeons (RCS), but RCS granted an option to Vandervell's trustees to repurchase the shares. In 1965, the trustees exercised the option using money from a children's trust fund. The trustees then held the repurchased shares, declaring they held them for the benefit of Vandervell's children. Vandervell's estate later claimed the shares were held on resulting trust for the estate, arguing no valid express trust of the shares had been created.
questions.stem: Under Re Vandervell (No 2), were the shares held on resulting trust for Vandervell's estate?

options.A: Yes, because the trustees had no authority to exercise the option using beneficiary funds without express instruction
options.B: No, because the trustees created a new express trust when they exercised the option using the children's trust fund money and manifested their intention to hold the shares for the children
options.C: Yes, because Vandervell's original intention to benefit the children could not be effectuated through an informal declaration
options.D: No, because the trustees' assumption of beneficial ownership automatically defeats any resulting trust claim
options.E: Yes, because the children's trust fund money should have remained segregated and could not be used for property acquisition

questions.explanation: B is correct. In Re Vandervell (No 2) [1974], the Court of Appeal held that when the trustees exercised the option to repurchase the shares using the children's trust fund money and manifested their intention to hold the shares for the children, a new express trust was created by the trustees' actions. The shares were not held on resulting trust for Vandervell's estate. The trustees' conduct—using the children's trust fund money and declaring they held for the children—was sufficient to create a valid express trust. A: The trustees exercised a power they had; the question is whether their conduct created a valid trust. C: Vandervell's original intention does not determine the legal effect; it is the trustees' subsequent actions that created the new trust. D: While this states the right outcome, it incorrectly attributes the trust to Vandervell's original intention rather than the trustees' later actions. E: The trustees had sufficient power to exercise the option and create a new trust with the children's trust fund money.

questions.scenario: Meera was appointed trustee under a trust deed that purported to settle a valuable painting for her nephew Hassan. However, the settlor died before transferring legal title to Meera, so the trust was never constituted. The painting passed to the settlor's executor, who mistakenly delivered it to Meera. Meera, knowing the trust had not been properly constituted, sold the painting for £50,000 and used the proceeds for her own business.
questions.stem: What fiduciary remedies are available against Meera for her actions?

options.A: Meera has committed no breach because the trust was not properly constituted, so she owed no fiduciary duty to Hassan
options.B: Although the trust was not formally constituted, Meera is accountable for the unauthorised profit because she assumed fiduciary responsibility by accepting the painting as trustee and is liable for the proceeds
options.C: Hassan can recover only the painting itself; because the trust was never constituted, he cannot claim equitable compensation for the proceeds
options.D: Meera must return only the painting's original value; appreciation in value is irrelevant to her liability
options.E: Meera owes no fiduciary duty; formal execution of the trust is an absolute precondition to fiduciary obligations

questions.explanation: B is correct. Although the trust was not formally constituted (legal title never properly vested in Meera as trustee), Meera received the painting in circumstances where she knew she was meant to hold it on trust for Hassan. By selling it and retaining the proceeds, she breached the fiduciary obligations she had assumed. Even where a trust is not properly constituted, a person who assumes fiduciary responsibility and then profits from property they were meant to hold on trust may be held accountable for unauthorised profit. A: The absence of formal constitution does not extinguish all equitable claims; fiduciary duties can arise from the assumption of responsibility. C: Hassan is not limited to recovery of the painting itself; equitable compensation for the proceeds is available. D: Appreciation in value is irrelevant; the claim is based on unauthorised profit from fiduciary position. E: Fiduciary duty does not require formal execution of the trust; it can arise from the assumption of the role and receipt of trust property.

questions.scenario: Sarah established a trust of £500,000 for the purpose of funding educational scholarships for underprivileged children in her local area. After 15 years, the purpose has been substantially fulfilled and only £50,000 remains unspent. The trust deed contains no provision for what should happen to surplus funds.
questions.stem: What is the most likely outcome for the £50,000 surplus under the law of resulting trusts?

options.A: The surplus is forfeited to the Crown as bona vacantia
options.B: The surplus is held on automatic resulting trust for Sarah (or her estate)
options.C: The trustees may distribute the surplus at their absolute discretion to any charitable purpose
options.D: The surplus must be paid to the beneficiaries pro rata according to their contributions
options.E: The surplus reverts to Sarah's heirs under the law of intestacy

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under the doctrine of automatic resulting trust, where an express trust disposes of only part of the equitable interest (here the scholarships consumed most but not all funds), the undisposed portion automatically results back to the settlor. A: Bona vacantia applies only where property is wholly unappropriated; here a partial failure triggers the RT. C: Trustees cannot arbitrarily dispose of surplus; the equitable interest must be determined by law. D: The trust beneficiaries (scholarship recipients) have no entitlement to surplus after the purpose is fulfilled. E: The property is held on trust for Sarah personally, not distributed under her intestacy.

questions.scenario: Marcus, a wealthy businessman, transfers his shareholding in a private company worth £2 million to his friend Daniel by deed. No written explanation is provided, and Marcus later insists he intended Daniel to hold the shares for his benefit, not as a gift. Daniel claims the transfer was a gift and refuses to return the shares.
questions.stem: In the absence of evidence of Marcus's true intention, what presumption applies to Marcus's transfer to Daniel?

options.A: A presumption of advancement because Marcus is older than Daniel
options.B: A presumption of resulting trust because the transfer was voluntary and gratuitous
options.C: A presumption of sham because no consideration was given
options.D: A presumption that Daniel holds the shares on trust because the deed must be construed contra proferentem
options.E: A presumption of absolute gift in favor of Daniel based on the formality of the deed

questions.explanation: B is correct. A presumed resulting trust arises where property is transferred voluntarily (not pursuant to an express trust) and there is no evidence of intention to benefit the transferee; the presumption is that the property is held on RT for the transferor. A: Presumption of advancement does not apply between friends; it applies only to father-child and husband-wife relationships. C: A sham is a pretence that the transfer had no real effect; here the transfer is real but the beneficial ownership is disputed. D: Contra proferentem is a rule of construction applied to ambiguous documents; it does not create a presumption in equity about resulting trusts. E: The formality of a deed does not rebut the presumption of resulting trust in the absence of express evidence of gift.

questions.scenario: James transferred 10,000 fully paid ordinary shares in his family company to his adult son Robert by deed. At the time of transfer, James and Robert had a good relationship and James expressed no formal condition or trust. Two years later, following a dispute with Robert, James claims the shares are held on resulting trust for him.
questions.stem: What is the effect of the presumption of advancement on James's claim?

options.A: The presumption of advancement is rebutted automatically because Robert is an adult
options.B: The presumption of advancement applies and creates a presumption that James intended to gift the shares to Robert
options.C: No presumption applies because the transfer was by formal deed
options.D: The presumption of advancement is rebutted by the mere fact that a dispute has arisen
options.E: The presumption of resulting trust overrides any presumption of advancement

questions.explanation: B is correct. The presumption of advancement is a rebuttable presumption that arises where a father transfers property to his child; it presumptively indicates an intention to gift, not to create a resulting trust for the father. James must adduce evidence of contrary intention to rebut this presumption. A: The presumption applies regardless of whether the child is an adult; adult status does not displace it. C: The formality of the deed is consistent with either a gift or a trust; it does not prevent the presumption from arising. D: The mere existence of a dispute does not rebut the presumption; James must provide affirmative evidence. E: The presumption of advancement is a specific response to transfers in certain relationships; there is no general presumption of RT that overrides it in father-child transfers.

questions.scenario: Vertex plc needed £500,000 to complete a major contract with a client. A bank advanced the money under a loan agreement expressly stating the money was for 'completion of the Vertex contract only'. Before the contract could be completed, Vertex's client pulled out. Vertex's business subsequently failed and entered insolvency. The bank seeks recovery of the £500,000 from the insolvency.
questions.stem: What is the legal basis for the bank's claim to recover the £500,000 from Vertex's insolvency proceedings?

options.A: The bank may recover as an unsecured creditor pro rata with other creditors
options.B: The bank may recover the full amount as a secured creditor with a fixed charge
options.C: The bank may recover the full amount because the money is held on a Quistclose resulting trust for the bank
options.D: The bank may only recover if it can prove Vertex committed fraud in obtaining the loan
options.E: The bank forfeits its claim because it cannot impose conditions on an unsecured loan

questions.explanation: C is correct. The Quistclose resulting trust applies where money is lent for a specific purpose and that purpose fails; the money is then held on RT for the lender, giving the lender priority recovery rights outside the insolvency. The bank advanced money for a specific purpose (completing the contract), the purpose failed, so a RT arises. A: If the bank were merely an unsecured creditor, it would rank pro rata; but the RT gives it priority. B: The bank did not take a fixed charge (which would require registration); instead the RT gives it equitable priority. D: Fraud is not required for a Quistclose trust; failure of purpose is sufficient. E: The bank's imposition of a purpose is the defining feature of a Quistclose trust and is enforceable.

questions.scenario: In 1961, Mr Vandervell transferred shares in his family company to the Royal College of Surgeons (RCS), but RCS granted an option to Vandervell's trustees to repurchase the shares. In 1965, the trustees exercised the option using money from a children's trust fund. The trustees then held the repurchased shares, declaring they held them for the benefit of Vandervell's children. Vandervell's estate later claimed the shares were held on resulting trust for the estate, arguing no valid express trust of the shares had been created.
questions.stem: Under Re Vandervell (No 2), were the shares held on resulting trust for Vandervell's estate?

options.A: Resulting trust for Vandervell's estate because the original trust deed was uncertain as to the terms
options.B: Express trust created by the trustees' actions in exercising the option with trust money
options.C: Resulting trust because Vandervell's original intention was not fully expressed
options.D: Express trust for Vandervell's grandchildren as Vandervell originally intended
options.E: Resulting trust because the trustees had no power to repurchase shares

questions.explanation: B is correct. In Re Vandervell (No 2) [1974], the Court of Appeal held that when the trustees exercised the option to repurchase the shares using the children's trust fund money and manifested their intention to hold the shares for the children, a new express trust was created by the trustees' actions. The shares were not held on resulting trust for Vandervell's estate. The trustees' conduct—using the children's trust fund money and declaring they held for the children—was sufficient to create a valid express trust. A: The original trust deed's uncertainty is irrelevant; the trustees created a new express trust by their subsequent actions. C: Vandervell's original intention does not determine the legal effect; it is the trustees' subsequent actions that created the new trust. D: While this states the right outcome, it incorrectly attributes the trust to Vandervell's original intention rather than the trustees' later actions. E: The trustees had sufficient power to exercise the option and create a new trust with the children's trust fund money.

questions.scenario: Oliver established a trust of £300,000 'for the benefit of such members of my family as my trustees shall in their discretion select'. The trust deed provided no guidance on who counted as 'family' or how the trustees should identify beneficiaries. When challenged, it was found the class of beneficiaries was conceptually uncertain. No distributions were ever made.
questions.stem: What happens to the £300,000 trust fund where the express trust fails due to uncertainty of objects?

options.A: The fund is distributed to Her Majesty's Treasury as bona vacantia
options.B: The fund is held on resulting trust for Oliver (or his estate)
options.C: The fund is distributed equally among all persons who might arguably be family members
options.D: The trustees may keep the fund as compensation for their services
options.E: The fund is held on trust for the Crown to be used for public purposes

questions.explanation: B is correct. Where an express trust wholly fails—here due to certainty of objects being unascertainable—the property is held on resulting trust for the settlor (or the settlor's estate). This principle was confirmed in Re Goldcorp and similar cases. A: Bona vacantia is a Crown claim to unclaimed property; it does not apply where an RT can arise.
