C: The trustees cannot distribute to an uncertain class; that is the very reason the trust fails. D: Trustees have no entitlement to trust funds; that would be a breach of trust. E: The Crown does not hold trusts for public purposes in this context; the property reverts to the settlor via an RT.

questions.scenario: The trustees of the Abbott Educational Fund held £150,000 for the purpose of providing financial assistance to deserving students. After twenty years of distributions, the fund's primary beneficiary (a young scholar) passed away. At that time, £25,000 remained unspent. The trust deed made no provision for what should occur to any surplus after the beneficiary's death.
questions.stem: How is the £25,000 surplus fund treated following the beneficiary's death?

options.A: The surplus is distributed to the deceased beneficiary's heirs
options.B: The surplus is forfeited to the Crown
options.C: The surplus is held on resulting trust for the original contributors to the fund
options.D: The trustees may apply the surplus to any purpose they consider beneficial
options.E: The surplus must be distributed equally to all students who previously received assistance

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under the principle established in Re Trusts of the Abbott Fund [1900], where a trust fund exists for a specific beneficiary or purpose and that beneficiary dies or purpose is exhausted, any surplus is held on resulting trust for the contributors/subscribers to the fund. A: The surplus is not part of the beneficiary's estate; it was held on trust for the contributor. B: Bona vacantia does not apply where a resulting trust arises for the fund's contributors. D: Trustees cannot unilaterally apply trust funds to new purposes; the equitable interest must be determined by law. E: Other beneficiaries of the same purpose have no claim to the surplus once the primary beneficiary has died.

questions.scenario: Natasha and Adrian purchased a residential property together with an oral agreement that despite taking legal title in Adrian's sole name (for mortgage purposes), Natasha would be the beneficial owner of 50%. Natasha made a substantial contribution to the purchase price and undertook significant renovations. Adrian later sought to deny Natasha's interest and sold the property without her consent.
questions.stem: Based on the facts, what type of constructive trust may be imposed in Natasha's favor?

options.A: An implied resulting trust based solely on Natasha's financial contribution
options.B: A constructive trust based on express common intention and detrimental reliance, as established in Lloyds Bank v Rosset
options.C: A presumption of advancement preventing any trust arising
options.D: A constructive trust based only on Natasha's detrimental conduct without any prior agreement
options.E: No constructive trust because the property is in Adrian's sole name

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991], a constructive trust arises where there is an express common intention (here the oral agreement to share beneficial ownership) coupled with detrimental reliance by the non-owner (Natasha's contributions and renovations). A: A resulting trust is based on voluntary transfer; here there is an express agreement about beneficial ownership. C: Presumption of advancement applies only to father-child and husband-wife relationships; it does not apply between unmarried cohabitants. D: Detrimental reliance alone is not sufficient; there must be an express or inferred common intention. E: The fact that Adrian holds legal title does not prevent a constructive trust from arising to protect Natasha's equitable interest.

questions.scenario: Victoria and William purchased a house in joint names. William paid 90% of the purchase price and made all mortgage payments, while Victoria paid 10% and contributed to household expenses. During their relationship, they made no express statements about their respective beneficial interests. The relationship later broke down and they disputed beneficial ownership.
questions.stem: How does the law approach quantification of beneficial interests where no express agreement exists?

options.A: Victoria automatically receives 50% as a joint legal owner, and William must prove a different intention
options.B: William receives 90% and Victoria 10% based strictly on their financial contributions
options.C: The court will infer a common intention from the whole course of dealing and may impute an intention if necessary, as established in Jones v Kernott
options.D: Victoria forfeits her interest entirely because she paid less than 50%
options.E: The court must award beneficial ownership based solely on who paid the mortgage

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under Stack v Dowden [2007] and Jones v Kernott [2011], where property is in joint names, the starting presumption is joint beneficial tenancy; this is displaced only by evidence of different common intention. Where actual common intention cannot be ascertained, the court may infer intention from the whole course of dealing or, if necessary, impute an intention as to what is fair. A: While Stack v Dowden creates a joint tenancy presumption, it is not irrebuttable; Jones v Kernott allows courts to infer and impute different intentions. B: Strict proportionality to contributions is not automatic; the court has discretion to infer or impute based on all circumstances. D: A minor contribution does not forfeit all interest; the court will determine beneficial shares in light of the parties' intentions. E: Mortgage payment alone does not determine all beneficial interests; the court considers the whole course of dealing.

questions.scenario: Lydia and Marcus exchanged contracts for the sale of a cottage worth £400,000. Before completion, Marcus attempted to terminate the contract and sell the property to a third party at a higher price. Lydia sought specific performance to enforce her right to purchase.
questions.stem: From the moment of exchange of contracts, what equitable interest does Lydia acquire?

options.A: No equitable interest until completion and transfer of legal title
options.B: A constructive trust of the property is imposed, making Lydia the beneficial owner from exchange of contracts, as established in Lysaght v Edwards
options.C: A mere personal right to claim damages for breach of contract
options.D: A resulting trust because Marcus held legal title prior to completion
options.E: A charge on the property securing only the deposit paid

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Lysaght v Edwards (1876), a constructive trust is imposed on the vendor's property for the benefit of the purchaser from the moment of exchange of contracts; the vendor becomes a trustee holding legal title for the purchaser's benefit because the contract is specifically enforceable. A: The exchange of contracts creates equitable interests before completion via the constructive trust doctrine. C: Although Lydia has a personal right to damages, the CT gives her an equitable proprietary interest superior to personal claims. D: A resulting trust arises from voluntary transfer or failure of trust; this CT arises from the specifically enforceable contract. E: A charge secures a loan; the CT gives Lydia the beneficial interest in the whole property.

questions.scenario: Oscar agreed orally with Patricia that if Patricia transferred her rental property to him, Oscar would hold it on trust for Patricia's benefit and she would continue to receive all rents. Patricia transferred the legal title by deed to Oscar. Oscar now claims absolute ownership and denies the oral agreement.
questions.stem: Can Patricia enforce the oral trust agreement against Oscar?

options.A: No, because the agreement was oral and s53(1)(b) LPA 1925 requires written evidence of trusts of land
options.B: Yes, a constructive trust is imposed to prevent Oscar using the Statute of Frauds as an instrument of fraud, per Rochefoucauld v Boustead
options.C: No, because Patricia transferred legal title, which vests all property rights in Oscar
options.D: Yes, but only if Patricia can prove Oscar acted with fraudulent intent
options.E: No, because oral trusts are never enforceable regardless of the circumstances

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Rochefoucauld v Boustead [1897], where property is transferred on an oral understanding that it will be held on trust, the legal owner cannot use the Statute of Frauds (or s53(1)(b) LPA) to deny the trust, because that would be using the statute as an instrument of fraud. The CT is imposed to enforce the oral agreement. A: While s53(1)(b) normally requires written evidence, s53(2) exempts constructive trusts from this requirement. C: Although Oscar has legal title, equity will impose a CT overriding his legal ownership. D: Fraud in the sense of dishonesty is not required; the use of the statute to deny an agreed trust suffices. E: Oral trusts can be enforced as CTs to prevent fraud; they are not automatically unenforceable.

questions.scenario: Rachel and Stephen jointly owned a plot of land. Rachel and Tanya agreed that Rachel would acquire an adjacent parcel at an upcoming auction, and Tanya would later purchase Rachel's interest in both parcels to develop them together. Rachel purchased the adjacent parcel at auction as agreed. Tanya now refuses to purchase Rachel's interest.
questions.stem: What equitable remedy may Rachel invoke under the Pallant v Morgan principle?

options.A: Rachel may enforce a specifically enforceable contract because the oral agreement was clear
options.B: Rachel may claim a resulting trust because she paid for the adjacent property
options.C: Rachel may enforce a constructive trust arising from the pre-acquisition agreement, as established in Pallant v Morgan [1953]
options.D: Rachel has only a personal claim for breach of contract and cannot recover the property
options.E: Rachel may impose a lien on Tanya's property to secure her investment

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under Pallant v Morgan [1953], where two parties agree that one will acquire property on terms that the other will obtain an interest, and the acquirer does obtain the property, a constructive trust is imposed in favor of the non-acquirer. The CT arises from the pre-acquisition agreement and conduct. A: While the agreement may be sufficiently certain, the specific remedy is the CT under Pallant v Morgan, not merely SP. B: A resulting trust requires voluntary transfer; here the CT doctrine is the appropriate mechanism. D: The Pallant v Morgan CT gives Rachel an equitable interest beyond a mere personal claim. E: A lien is not the appropriate remedy; the CT gives Rachel an equitable interest in the acquired property itself.

questions.scenario: Trevor's will contained a gift of £100,000 to his friend Vincent 'absolutely'. Before executing the will, Trevor privately told Vincent that the £100,000 was to be held on trust for Trevor's mistress, Carol, and Vincent accepted this obligation. Trevor later died. The will was read and disclosed the gift to Vincent, but not the trust.
questions.stem: What type of secret trust is this, and on what basis is it enforced?

options.A: A half-secret trust, because the will reveals both the gift and the trust obligation
options.B: A fully secret trust, because the trust obligation was communicated privately and not disclosed in the will, and is enforced as a constructive trust to prevent fraud per Ottaway v Norman
options.C: A resulting trust, because the will is silent as to the intended beneficial ownership
options.D: A half-secret trust that requires written evidence under s53(1)(b)
options.E: No trust at all, because secret trusts violate the rules of testamentary formality

questions.explanation: B is correct. A fully secret trust arises where a gift appears absolute on the face of the will, but the testator has communicated a trust obligation to the legatee in private and the legatee has accepted; it is enforced as a CT to prevent fraud and takes effect outside the will (per Ottaway v Norman [1972]). A: A half-secret trust is where the will reveals that a trust exists but not its terms; here the will shows an absolute gift. C: A resulting trust would apply if no trust had been agreed; here an agreed trust is being enforced. D: s53(2) LPA exempts CTs from writing requirements; the written will is not necessary for enforcement. E: Secret trusts are enforceable precisely because they are treated as CTs to prevent fraud; they bypass testamentary formality.

questions.scenario: Trustee Marcus held a fund of £100,000 for his beneficiary nephew Tom. Without authorisation, Marcus invested £80,000 in a speculative tech venture that collapsed, leaving only £5,000. The fund should have grown to £120,000 based on its agreed investment strategy.
questions.stem: What is the measure of Marcus's liability to Tom for this unauthorised investment breach?

options.A: £115,000, representing the difference between what the fund should be worth and what it is now worth
options.B: £80,000, being the full amount that Marcus invested without authority
options.C: £115,000, being the gross loss across all the investments in the fund
options.D: £100,000, representing the original principal amount only
options.E: £5,000, being the diminished value of the investment

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under Target Holdings v Redferns [1996] and AIB v Mark Redler [2014], the trustee is liable to restore the trust fund to the position it would have been in but for the breach. The fund should be worth £120,000; it is now worth £5,000. Marcus must compensate for the net loss of £115,000. B is incorrect because it measures gross breach amount, not net loss caused. C restates the correct figure but with incorrect reasoning. D is incorrect because the original principal is already lost; compensation concerns the shortfall from expected performance. E is incorrect as it measures only what remains, not the loss suffered.

questions.scenario: Trustee Priya administered a trust with a clause stating: 'The trustee shall not be liable for any loss arising from investments or mismanagement, unless the trustee has acted dishonestly.' Priya made a negligent investment decision, genuinely believing it was sound, that resulted in a significant loss to the trust fund.
questions.stem: Can the court relieve Priya from liability under section 61 of the Trustee Act 1925?

options.A: No, because the trust instrument contains an exemption clause that already protects negligent trustees
options.B: Possibly yes, if the court finds Priya acted honestly and reasonably and it is fair to excuse her, despite the exemption clause being present
options.C: Yes, automatically, because section 61 overrides all exemption clauses in trust instruments
options.D: No, because section 61 only applies to fraud, not to negligence
options.E: Yes, because Priya genuinely believed her decision was sound

questions.explanation: B is correct. Section 61 of the Trustee Act 1925 permits the court to relieve a trustee from liability if the trustee acted honestly, reasonably, and ought fairly to be excused—even if an exemption clause exists, the court has discretion. A is incorrect because exemption clauses, though valid, do not automatically prevent s61 relief; s61 remains available as a separate equitable doctrine. C is incorrect because s61 does not automatically override exemption clauses; relief is discretionary based on the trustee's conduct. D is incorrect because s61 applies to all breaches of trust, including negligent breaches, not only fraud. E is incorrect because honest belief alone is insufficient; the trustee must have acted reasonably and the court must find it fair to excuse the breach.

questions.scenario: In 2014, trustee David made an unauthorised investment that lost £50,000 from his beneficiary's trust. The beneficiary, Sarah, did not discover the breach until 2020 when she reviewed the trust accounts in detail. She sued David for equitable compensation in 2024.
questions.stem: What is the status of Sarah's claim under the Limitation Act 1980?

options.A: The claim is time-barred because more than 6 years have passed since the breach in 2014
options.B: The claim is time-barred because more than 3 years have passed since discovery in 2020
options.C: The claim is not time-barred; section 21 Limitation Act 1980 imposes no time limit for actions to recover trust property or its proceeds from the trustee
options.D: The claim succeeds if Sarah can prove David acted dishonestly; otherwise it is time-barred
options.E: The claim is time-barred unless Sarah can show David concealed the breach

questions.explanation: C is correct. Section 21(1) Limitation Act 1980 provides that there is NO limitation period for actions (a) for fraud or fraudulent breach of trust, or (b) to recover trust property or its proceeds from the trustee. Sarah's claim to recover compensation falls within this exception. A is incorrect because the 6-year limitation under s21(3) applies only to breaches that do not fall within s21(1) exceptions. B is incorrect because the Limitation Act 1980 does not impose a 3-year period for equitable compensation claims. D is incorrect because the absence of limitation applies to any action to recover trust property, not only dishonest breaches. E is incorrect because the statute does not require concealment; the exemption from limitation is automatic for claims against trustees to recover trust property.

questions.scenario: Trustees Ahmed and Beth jointly misappropriated £100,000 from a trust fund. Ahmed persuaded Beth to go along with the scheme, though Beth was genuinely hesitant. The beneficiary sued both, and both were found liable. Ahmed is significantly more culpable for orchestrating the breach.
questions.stem: What remedy is available regarding the liability between Ahmed and Beth under the Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978?

options.A: Ahmed and Beth must each pay £50,000 to the beneficiary as equal joint tortfeasors
options.B: Beth can recover a contribution from Ahmed in proportion to their respective culpability, which the court will assess as just and equitable
options.C: Beth cannot recover any contribution because she was a party to the breach
options.D: Ahmed is solely liable and Beth is automatically relieved as the less culpable trustee
options.E: The beneficiary can choose to recover the full amount from whichever trustee is wealthier

questions.explanation: B is correct. Section 1 of the Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978 allows any person liable in respect of damage to recover contribution from any other person liable for the same damage; the court must assess the contribution as it finds just and equitable having regard to the degree of culpability. A is incorrect because contributions are not automatic 50/50 splits; they depend on culpability. C is incorrect because the Act applies to persons liable in respect of the same damage, and contribution is a right between co-liable parties regardless of breach status. D is incorrect because the Act does not automatically relieve less culpable parties; it allows contribution between them. E is incorrect because the beneficiary's right is against both trustees jointly; the apportionment among trustees is governed by the Act's contribution mechanism.

questions.scenario: Trustee Lisa sought permission from adult beneficiary Oliver before investing £50,000 of the trust fund in a high-risk venture. Oliver, fully understanding the risk, signed a written consent form. The investment failed and lost £40,000. Oliver later sued Lisa for breach of trust.
questions.stem: What is the likely outcome of Oliver's claim?

options.A: Oliver will succeed because trustees cannot rely on beneficiary consent as a defence to breach of trust
options.B: Oliver will fail because he gave free and informed consent to the investment, which operates as a defence under Re Pauling's Settlement Trusts
options.C: Oliver will succeed because even with consent, the investment was objectively too risky
options.D: Oliver will fail only if Lisa can prove that the investment was reasonable, regardless of consent
options.E: Oliver will succeed because written consent does not bind beneficiaries to waive breach claims

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Re Pauling's Settlement Trusts [1964], a beneficiary who has given free and informed consent to, or concurred in, a breach of trust cannot subsequently sue the trustee for that breach. Oliver's written consent, given with full understanding, operates as a defence. A is incorrect because consent is a well-established equitable defence when given freely and with full information. C is incorrect because the objective riskiness is irrelevant if consent was genuinely given; the defence operates regardless of outcome. D is incorrect because the defence depends on consent, not on the objective reasonableness of the investment. E is incorrect because written consent is highly probative of the beneficiary's agreement and binding on beneficiaries for future claims.

questions.scenario: Trustee Carlos made two unauthorised investments on behalf of his trust. Investment A lost £30,000. Investment B, also unauthorised but of a completely different type, profited by £15,000. Carlos seeks to set off the £15,000 profit against the £30,000 loss to reduce his liability.
questions.stem: Can Carlos set off the profit from Investment B against the loss from Investment A?

options.A: Yes, because both are unauthorised investments and the trustee must account for net profit and loss
options.B: No, because set-off is not generally available between separate transactions unless they are so connected they form one single dealing
options.C: Yes, if both investments were made on the same day with funds from the same source
options.D: No, because a loss cannot be offset by an unrelated profit in any circumstances
options.E: Yes, because equitable compensation requires looking at the overall trust portfolio performance

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Bartlett v Barclays Bank Trust Co [1980], set-off is not generally available; a profit on one transaction cannot offset a loss on another unless the transactions are so connected that they form one single dealing. Here, two separate, unrelated investments cannot be set off. A is incorrect because the mere fact of both being unauthorised does not permit set-off of profits against losses. C is incorrect because timing and fund source do not justify set-off between unrelated transactions. D is incorrect because the rule is not absolute; set-off is possible for connected transactions, but these are not. E is incorrect because equitable compensation is assessed on the basis of restoring the trust fund, not by treating all transactions as a single portfolio where profits offset losses.

questions.scenario: Trustee Sophie misapplied £80,000 from a trust fund and invested it in property in her own name. The property is now worth £150,000. Sophie is currently insolvent and has few other assets. The beneficiary now seeks to recover the misapplied funds.
questions.stem: What are the relative advantages of pursuing a proprietary claim versus a personal claim in Sophie's insolvency?

options.A: A personal claim is preferable because it is faster to resolve and gives priority in insolvency
options.B: A proprietary claim is preferable because it allows the beneficiary to claim the specific asset and benefit from its appreciation, and has priority over general creditors in insolvency
options.C: Both claims are equivalent in value and the beneficiary should pursue whichever is administratively simpler
options.D: A proprietary claim is only available if the beneficiary can prove Sophie committed fraud
options.E: A personal claim is preferable because it allows recovery of equitable compensation rather than being limited to the original fund value

questions.explanation: B is correct. A proprietary claim tracks the misapplied trust property into the asset (here, the property), allowing the beneficiary to claim the specific asset, benefit from any increase in value (£150,000 rather than £80,000), and claim priority over general creditors in insolvency. A is incorrect because personal claims do not confer priority in insolvency, whereas proprietary claims do; personal claims rank with general creditors. C is incorrect because the claims are not equivalent: proprietary claims offer capital appreciation and priority, personal claims do not. D is incorrect because proprietary claims require only traceability of the misapplied property, not proof of fraud. E is incorrect because a personal claim does not yield equitable compensation above the original fund; it yields only compensation equal to the loss suffered.

questions.scenario: Trustee Neil transferred £200,000 from the trust to his business associate, a company called Zenith Ltd, telling the company it was a loan against future projects. Zenith Ltd had no knowledge of the trust relationship and genuinely believed it was a commercial loan from Neil personally. The transfer was in fact a breach of trust.
questions.stem: Under the test in BCCI v Akindele, can the beneficiary pursue Zenith Ltd for knowing receipt?

options.A: Yes, because Zenith Ltd received trust property in breach of trust, regardless of its knowledge
options.B: No, because Zenith Ltd did not have knowledge of the trust breach, and the single test of knowing receipt requires knowledge making retention unconscionable
options.C: Yes, if Neil is insolvent, the beneficiary can pursue any recipient of the funds
options.D: No, because Zenith Ltd received the funds as a loan, not a gift, so the doctrine does not apply
options.E: Yes, because the company received funds that originally came from a trust

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under BCCI v Akindele [2001], knowing receipt requires three elements: (1) receipt of trust property or its traceable proceeds, (2) in breach of trust/fiduciary duty, and (3) knowledge making retention unconscionable (the single test). Zenith Ltd's genuine belief that it was a commercial loan means it lacked the requisite knowledge, so it cannot be liable for knowing receipt. A is incorrect because knowledge is a necessary element of the doctrine; mere receipt is insufficient. C is incorrect because insolvency of the transferor does not trigger knowing receipt liability against recipients with innocent knowledge. D is incorrect because the form of the receipt (loan vs gift) does not determine whether the doctrine applies; what matters is whether the recipient received property with unconscionable knowledge. E is incorrect because simple receipt of funds that originated in a trust, without the requisite knowledge, does not suffice.

questions.scenario: Geoffrey and his business partner Priya orally agreed that after Geoffrey's death, all his business assets would be held on resulting trust for Priya. At the time, the business was unregistered land and included valuable equipment. Geoffrey died six months later. Geoffrey's executors seek to argue that the oral agreement is void for failure to comply with section 53(1) LPA 1925.
questions.stem: Is the oral resulting trust agreement enforceable despite lacking written evidence?

options.A: No, because resulting trusts of land must be evidenced in writing under section 53(1) LPA 1925
options.B: Yes, because resulting trusts are exempted from the writing requirements of section 53(1) by section 53(2) LPA 1925, even though the assets include land
options.C: No, unless Priya can show part-performance or detrimental reliance on the agreement
options.D: Yes, but only if Priya can prove the oral agreement was witnessed by an independent third party
options.E: No, because oral trusts are only valid for personal property, not for business interests including land

questions.explanation: B is correct. Section 53(2) LPA 1925 provides that resulting, implied and constructive trusts are exempt from the writing requirements in section 53(1). An oral resulting trust is therefore valid even where the property includes or consists of land. A is incorrect because s53(2) explicitly exempts resulting trusts from the s53(1) writing requirement. C is incorrect because part-performance and detrimental reliance are equitable doctrines from pre-1989 law that have been superseded; the s53(2) exemption provides direct validity. D is incorrect because s53(2) does not require witnesses; the exemption applies to any resulting trust, oral or otherwise. E is incorrect because s53(2) applies regardless of the nature of the property (land or personal property).
